proposals.title: Concordant Fetal Growth Restriction in Monochorionic Twins: A Greater Contributor to Preterm Birth and Adverse Perinatal Outcome than Birthweight Discordance
proposals.motivation: In monochorionic (MC) twin pregnancies, clinical surveillance and intervention algorithms are heavily focused on identifying and managing significant inter-twin birthweight discordance, a known risk factor for adverse outcome. However, this focus may overlook the specific, shared placental insufficiency leading to concordant growth restriction in both twins, a condition whose independent contribution to severe preterm birth and composite adverse outcome is poorly quantified. A precise understanding of the primary drivers of MC-specific risk is essential to refine antenatal care pathways and improve outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: In monochorionic diamniotic twin pregnancies, concordant fetal growth restriction (estimated fetal weight <10th percentile in both twins) is a stronger independent predictor of spontaneous preterm birth <32 weeks and a composite severe adverse perinatal outcome than isolated significant birthweight discordance (>25%).
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of MC twin pregnancies managed at the Harris Birthright Research Centre from 2010-2025, with detailed serial ultrasound biometry from 16 weeks to delivery. The primary exposure will be categorized as: 1) Concordant FGR (both twins <10th percentile), 2) Discordant growth (one twin <10th percentile with >25% discordance), 3) Discordance only (>25% with both twins >10th percentile), and 4) Concordant appropriate growth. Using multivariable Cox proportional hazards and logistic regression models, we will analyze the independent association of each category with the primary outcome (spontaneous preterm birth <32 weeks) and a composite secondary outcome (perinatal mortality, severe neonatal morbidity), adjusting for gestational age at diagnosis, preeclampsia, and other confounders.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, risk-stratified data to challenge the clinical primacy of discordance as the principal MC risk marker. It will quantify the specific burden of concordant FGR, potentially redefining high-risk phenotypes. The findings will directly inform the development of more nuanced monitoring protocols and the design of future intervention trials aimed at prolonging gestation in MC pregnancies with shared placental insufficiency.
proposals.novelty: While discordance is well-studied, this proposal systematically isolates and compares the risk of concordant growth restriction—a condition often subsumed within broader analyses—against discordance, using a large, deeply phenotyped MC cohort and time-to-event analysis to account for gestational age at delivery.

proposals.title: Quantitative Analysis of Placental Vascular Anastomoses and Their Association with Concordant Fetal Growth Restriction in Monochorionic Diamniotic Twins: A Prospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Monochorionic diamniotic (MCDA) twins are at high risk of adverse outcomes, including preterm birth and fetal growth restriction (FGR). While selective FGR in MCDA twins is well-studied in the context of twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome (TTTS), the placental mechanisms underlying concordant FGR—where both twins are growth-restricted—are poorly understood. This condition poses a significant clinical challenge for timing of birth and risk stratification, as current management is largely extrapolated from singleton pregnancies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in MCDA pregnancies with concordant FGR, the pattern of placental vascular anastomoses (arterio-arterial, veno-venous, and arterio-venous) is quantitatively distinct, characterized by a paucity of compensatory anastomoses, leading to globally insufficient placental share and function for both fetuses.
proposals.method: This prospective cohort study will recruit women with MCDA twins between 16-20 weeks of gestation. Serial ultrasound assessments will monitor fetal growth, with concordant FGR defined as estimated fetal weight <10th percentile in both twins at ≥26 weeks. Placentas will be collected at birth and subjected to ex-vivo injection with colored dyes to delineate vascular territories and anastomoses. Using high-resolution digital photography and custom image analysis software (e.g., ImageJ with vascular tracing algorithms), we will quantify the number, type, diameter, and functional angio-architecture of anastomoses. These placental metrics will be compared between cases of concordant FGR and appropriately grown MCDA controls, adjusting for gestational age at birth and maternal factors.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed, quantitative characterization of the placental vascular phenotype associated with concordant FGR in MCDA twins. It will clarify whether this condition represents a distinct placental entity from TTTS and isolated selective FGR. The findings aim to inform future risk prediction models and could guide the development of tailored surveillance protocols for this high-risk subgroup.
proposals.novelty: While placental anastomoses are central to MCDA twin complications, research has focused on discordant growth and TTTS. This proposal systematically investigates the neglected but clinically significant problem of concordant FGR, applying rigorous quantitative morphometric analysis to the entire placental angio-architecture rather than focusing on individual anastomotic types in isolation.

proposals.title: Re-evaluating the 34-Week Threshold for Late-Onset Preeclampsia: A Data-Driven Analysis of Predictive Model Performance and Clinical Utility
proposals.motivation: Current clinical and research paradigms, including the ASPRE trial, dichotomize preeclampsia into 'preterm' (<37 weeks) and 'term' (≥37 weeks), with 'late-onset' often defined as ≥34 weeks. This 34-week cutoff, while operationally useful, is largely arbitrary and may not reflect the true biological continuum of the disease. A rigid threshold could misclassify patients, potentially leading to suboptimal risk stratification and management decisions, particularly for women delivering between 34 and 37 weeks of gestation.
proposals.hypothesis: The predictive performance and clinical utility of the established first-trimester combined screening model for preeclampsia (using maternal factors, biophysical, and biochemical markers) are not optimal at the fixed 34-week cutoff, and a data-driven, gestational age-specific threshold will improve risk classification and better align with perinatal outcomes.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of individual patient-level data from the ASPRE trial and related cohorts. Using the existing preeclampsia risk algorithm, we will calculate continuous gestational age-specific risks. Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) analysis and net benefit analysis from Decision Curve Analysis (DCA) will be performed across the gestational age spectrum from 34 to 41 weeks. We will identify the gestational age at which model discrimination (AUC) and clinical utility (net benefit) are maximized, comparing the proposed threshold against the standard 34-week cutoff.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide evidence to either validate or challenge the conventional 34-week threshold, moving towards a more nuanced, gestational age-informed definition of 'late-onset' disease. It will offer a framework for optimizing the timing of interventions, such as aspirin prophylaxis or surveillance protocols, based on individualized risk trajectories rather than a binary cutoff.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has validated the first-trimester screening model, no study has systematically tested the optimal gestational age cutoff for defining the disease it aims to predict. This analysis shifts the focus from validating a fixed threshold to letting the data determine the most clinically meaningful point of separation.

proposals.title: Dynamic Risk Assessment for Preterm Preeclampsia in Twin Pregnancies: A Time-to-Event Analysis from 11-13 to 36 Weeks of Gestation
proposals.motivation: Twin pregnancies carry a significantly elevated risk of preterm preeclampsia (PE), leading to severe maternal and perinatal morbidity. Current screening, based on the Fetal Medicine Foundation algorithm at 11-13 weeks, provides a static risk estimate, but the dynamic nature of pregnancy and the availability of serial biomarker measurements are not utilized. A method to update individual risk profiles throughout gestation could enable more personalized surveillance and timely intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying time-to-event (survival) analysis to longitudinal maternal characteristics and biomarker data in twin pregnancies will generate dynamic, gestational-age-specific hazard functions that more accurately predict the risk of developing preterm PE than a single first-trimester assessment.
proposals.method: We will analyze a prospective cohort of twin pregnancies with serial measurements (e.g., mean arterial pressure, uterine artery pulsatility index, serum PlGF) taken at routine visits from 11-13 to 36 weeks. Using Cox proportional hazards regression with time-dependent covariates, we will model the instantaneous hazard (risk) of preterm PE diagnosis. The Prentice, Williams, and Peterson total time model will be employed to account for recurrent events (e.g., serial measurements). Model performance will be assessed using time-dependent ROC curves and Brier scores.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first validated, dynamic risk prediction model for preterm PE in twins, moving beyond static first-trimester screening. It will provide clinicians with a tool for updating individual patient risk at each antenatal visit, potentially guiding the timing and intensity of monitoring. The methodology will establish a framework for applying survival analysis to other pregnancy complications.
proposals.novelty: While survival analysis is established in oncology, its application to dynamically model the evolving risk of a pregnancy complication like preeclampsia, particularly in high-risk twin gestations, is novel. This approach directly addresses the temporal nature of the disease process, unlike previous cross-sectional or static risk models.

proposals.title: The Impact of Low Fetal Fraction on the Performance of First-Trimester Combined Screening for Preterm Preeclampsia in Singleton and Twin Pregnancies: A Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: As of 2025, first-trimester combined screening for preterm preeclampsia (PE), as pioneered by the Fetal Medicine Foundation and validated in the ASPRE trial, is standard practice. This screening relies on maternal characteristics, biophysical markers, and biochemical markers (PAPP-A, PlGF), but its performance is predicated on obtaining sufficient analyte levels. Low fetal fraction (FF), a known cause of test failure in cell-free DNA screening, may also reflect placental dysfunction and thus directly impact the accuracy of PE risk algorithms, particularly in twins who are at higher baseline risk and for whom screening performance is less well-defined.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pregnancies with low fetal fraction (<4% or the lowest decile for gestation) will have a systematically altered biochemical profile (lower PAPP-A and/or PlGF) leading to an underestimation of true risk by the FMF algorithm, resulting in a higher than expected rate of preterm PE among screen-negative cases, with this effect being more pronounced in twin pregnancies.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using the extensive pre-2026 database of the Fetal Medicine Foundation, linking first-trimester screening data (11-13 weeks) with pregnancy outcome data. We will identify all singleton and twin pregnancies with recorded PAPP-A and PlGF values and known outcomes. Fetal fraction will be estimated using a pre-2026 validated method correlating PAPP-A multiples of the median (MoM) with gestational age and maternal weight. Screening performance (detection and false-positive rates) for preterm PE (<37 weeks) will be compared between low-FF and normal-FF cohorts using the established FMF risk algorithm. Subgroup analysis will be performed for singletons and twins, and logistic regression will assess FF as an independent predictor of screening failure.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first evidence-based thresholds for fetal fraction adequacy in PE screening. It will quantify the potential for risk misclassification in a vulnerable subgroup, informing clinical guidelines on test interpretation and the need for enhanced surveillance. For twin pregnancies, it will clarify a major confounder in screening performance, directly addressing a key research gap in Professor Nicolaides's work on risk assessment in multiple gestations.
proposals.novelty: While low FF is a recognized issue in NIPT, its specific impact on the biochemical placental profile used for PE risk calculation has not been systematically investigated. This study inverts the standard assumption of analyte sufficiency to explicitly examine screening failures and their clinical consequences.

proposals.title: A Time-to-Verification Analysis Framework for Estimating cfDNA Test Performance with Incomplete Pregnancy Outcome Data: A Statistical Method Adapted from Survival Analysis
proposals.motivation: Validating cell-free DNA (cfDNA) screening tests for fetal aneuploidy requires complete pregnancy outcome data, which is often unavailable due to loss to follow-up, terminations, or late-onset diagnoses. This leads to biased estimates of sensitivity and specificity and hinders robust test evaluation. A statistical method is urgently needed to incorporate partial follow-up data without discarding valuable information from pregnancies without a final confirmed diagnosis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting right-censoring methods from survival analysis to model the time from cfDNA test to diagnostic verification will provide valid, less biased estimates of test performance metrics compared to analyses restricted to complete cases. This framework will efficiently use all available follow-up data.
proposals.method: We will develop a statistical framework where the 'event' is a confirmed diagnostic outcome (e.g., karyotype from CVS/amniocentesis or postnatal assessment). For each pregnancy, we define the 'time' to this event from the cfDNA test date. Subjects without a final outcome by the study end are right-censored. We will adapt Kaplan-Meier estimators and Cox proportional hazards models to estimate cumulative incidence of a positive diagnosis, stratified by cfDNA result. Sensitivity and specificity will be derived from these time-to-verification curves at relevant clinical timepoints (e.g., 20 weeks, term).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, practical statistical tool for the validation of prenatal screening tests, increasing the statistical power and reducing bias in real-world evaluations. It will establish a formal methodology for handling the common problem of incomplete outcome data in prenatal screening research, directly benefiting large-scale audit and quality assurance programs.
proposals.novelty: While survival analysis is standard in other fields, its application to the specific problem of cfDNA test validation with censored outcome data is novel. This approach moves beyond the simplistic complete-case analysis, formally incorporating the timing and probability of outcome verification into performance estimates.

proposals.title: Temporal and Population-Specific Calibration of Maternal Age-Related Risk for Preterm Birth and Preeclampsia in Twin Pregnancies
proposals.motivation: Current screening algorithms for preterm birth and preeclampsia in twins, such as those developed in the ASPRE trial, incorporate maternal age as a static, population-invariant risk factor. This is problematic because secular trends in maternal age at childbirth, assisted reproductive technology use, and population health may alter the true risk profile associated with age over time and across different cohorts, potentially reducing the predictive accuracy of established models.
proposals.hypothesis: The relative risk and attributable fraction for adverse perinatal outcomes (specifically spontaneous preterm birth <34 weeks and preterm preeclampsia) associated with maternal age are not fixed but vary significantly across different temporal eras and distinct population cohorts of twin pregnancies.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective analysis of large, multi-center databases of twin pregnancies from 2000-2025. Using individual patient-level data, we will employ time-stratified and population-stratified logistic regression models, with maternal age as a continuous variable, to estimate outcome-specific risks. The analysis will test for interaction effects between age and era/population. Model calibration will be assessed using observed-to-expected ratios and calibration plots within each stratum, comparing the performance of static vs. recalibrated age coefficients.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide evidence-based, dynamic coefficients for maternal age in twin pregnancy risk prediction models. It will deliver a framework for periodically recalibrating existing algorithms, like the Fetal Medicine Foundation's combined screening for preeclampsia, to maintain accuracy across time and populations, thereby improving personalized risk assessment and timing of interventions such as cervical cerclage or timed birth.
proposals.novelty: While the need for model updating is recognized in principle, this study directly quantifies the temporal and population-based variance of a fundamental, non-modifiable risk factor (maternal age) within the specific high-risk context of twin gestation, challenging its treatment as a constant in clinical practice.

proposals.title: A National Implementation Model for First-Trimester Nuchal Translucency Screening: Protocol for a Stepped-Wedge Cluster Randomized Trial and Health Economic Evaluation
proposals.motivation: First-trimester combined screening, integrating nuchal translucency (NT) measurement with maternal serum markers, is a cornerstone of modern prenatal care, enabling early risk assessment for chromosomal anomalies and, as demonstrated by the ASPRE trial, effective prevention of preterm preeclampsia. However, its implementation is inconsistent, creating significant health inequities. A standardized national program is needed to ensure uniform access, quality, and integration with subsequent pregnancy management pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: A structured, multi-center implementation model for first-trimester NT screening, incorporating standardized training, quality assurance, and integrated risk software, will significantly improve screening uptake, measurement quality (as defined by Fetal Medicine Foundation criteria), and the effective triage of high-risk women for targeted interventions compared to existing ad-hoc practices.
proposals.method: We propose a stepped-wedge cluster randomized trial across 20-30 UK NHS maternity units. The intervention is a bundled implementation package: 1) Centralized sonographer certification via the FMF online platform, 2) Deployment of unified risk-calculation software (e.g., Astraia) integrating NT, biochemistry, and maternal factors for preeclampsia risk, and 3) A defined clinical pathway for screen-positive outcomes. Primary outcomes are the proportion of eligible women receiving a valid FMF-certified NT measurement and the detection rate for trisomy 21. A parallel health economic analysis will model long-term costs and outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first robust evidence for the effectiveness and cost-effectiveness of a national NT screening framework. It will yield a scalable implementation blueprint, including training protocols, quality metrics, and IT infrastructure requirements, directly informing public health policy. The study will also generate novel data on the population-wide impact of integrating first-trimester preeclampsia prevention into a standardized screening program.
proposals.novelty: While NT screening is well-established, the systematic design and trial-based evaluation of a *national implementation model* is novel. This moves beyond proving clinical efficacy to solving the practical health systems challenge of equitable, high-quality delivery, integrating multiple risk assessments (aneuploidy, preeclampsia, preterm birth) into a single scalable pathway.

proposals.title: External Validation of the Fetal Medicine Foundation First-Trimester Preterm Birth and Preeclampsia Prediction Models in Diverse Global Populations: A Multi-Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: First-trimester prediction models for adverse pregnancy outcomes like preterm birth and preeclampsia, such as those developed by the Fetal Medicine Foundation (FMF) and validated in the ASPRE trial, have shown promise in enabling targeted prevention strategies. However, their performance has primarily been assessed in well-resourced, largely European populations. It is unknown whether these algorithms, which combine maternal characteristics, biophysical markers, and biochemical assays, maintain their predictive accuracy and clinical utility in diverse global settings, particularly in low-resource countries with different population characteristics, healthcare practices, and disease prevalence.
proposals.hypothesis: The FMF first-trimester prediction algorithms for preterm birth (<34 weeks) and preterm preeclampsia will demonstrate significantly lower discrimination (AUC) and require recalibration when applied to external cohorts from low- and middle-income countries (LMICs) compared to their performance in the original development and validation studies.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective, multi-cohort external validation study using existing pregnancy data from 8-10 diverse global sites, including at least five in LMICs. We will apply the exact FMF algorithms (combining maternal factors, mean arterial pressure, uterine artery Doppler pulsatility index, and placental growth factor) to each cohort's first-trimester data. Primary outcomes are preterm preeclampsia (<37 weeks) and spontaneous preterm birth (<34 weeks). We will assess model performance by calculating the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC), calibration plots, and decision curve analysis for each cohort separately, comparing results to published FMF benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive evidence on the generalizability of leading first-trimester prediction models across diverse populations. It will identify specific populations where models fail and quantify the need for local recalibration or new model development. The findings will directly inform global guidelines on the equitable implementation of first-trimester screening and prevention programs.
proposals.novelty: While external validation is a known need, no pre-2026 study has systematically tested these sophisticated, multi-parameter FMF algorithms across a wide range of LMIC settings with varying antenatal care pathways, which is critical for determining their true global health impact beyond specialized referral centers.

proposals.title: Integrative Multi-Omic Profiling to Link Placental Dysfunction to Maternal Systemic Inflammation in Preterm Preeclampsia
proposals.motivation: The two-stage model of preeclampsia posits that abnormal placentation (Stage 1) triggers a maternal systemic inflammatory response (Stage 2), but the precise mechanistic link remains unclear. This gap hinders the development of targeted interventions to prevent progression from placental insufficiency to clinical disease, particularly in pregnancies at high risk of preterm preeclampsia, a major cause of maternal and perinatal morbidity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, quantifiable signatures of placental oxidative stress and syncytiotrophoblast stress, measured in maternal blood, directly initiate and calibrate the maternal endothelial and inflammatory cascade observed in preterm preeclampsia.
proposals.method: We will conduct a nested case-control study within a high-risk cohort screened at 11-13 weeks. Using serial maternal blood samples (from screening, 20, 28 weeks), we will perform integrated multi-omics: cell-free RNA sequencing to assess placental-specific gene expression, targeted mass spectrometry for oxidized lipids and proteins, and a multiplex cytokine/anti-angiogenic factor panel. Machine learning (e.g., Random Forest regression) will be used to model temporal trajectories and identify the most predictive early omic features of later systemic inflammation and clinical diagnosis, adjusting for known risk factors.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first longitudinal, multi-omic map of the transition from placental to systemic disease in preeclampsia. It will identify novel, early circulating biomarkers of pathological progression and clarify which specific placental signals are critical drivers of the maternal syndrome, informing future therapeutic strategies.
proposals.novelty: While the two-stage model is accepted, this study uniquely applies serial, integrative multi-omics in a meticulously phenotyped, high-risk cohort to dynamically model the causal pathway, moving beyond static associations to define the pathogenic sequence.

proposals.title: A Machine Learning Framework for Individualized Preterm Preeclampsia Risk Stratification: Adapting Cardiovascular Endothelial Dysfunction Models to Obstetrics
proposals.motivation: Pre-eclampsia, a leading cause of maternal and perinatal morbidity, shares a pathophysiological foundation of endothelial dysfunction with cardiovascular disease. Current first-trimester screening models, like the FMF algorithm, are effective but primarily linear. Given the complex, multifactorial nature of pre-eclampsia, especially preterm pre-eclampsia, there is a need for more sophisticated, individualized risk prediction to guide targeted monitoring and intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: Machine learning algorithms, specifically those developed to model cardiovascular risk from complex, longitudinal data, can be adapted to maternal biomarker and ultrasound data to improve the prediction of preterm pre-eclampsia, by more accurately capturing its non-linear endothelial dysfunction signature.
proposals.method: We will utilize a retrospective cohort of women screened in the first trimester (11-13 weeks), incorporating maternal characteristics, mean arterial pressure, uterine artery pulsatility index, and serum biomarkers (PlGF, PAPP-A). We will adapt and train established cardiovascular risk prediction architectures, such as gradient boosting machines (e.g., XGBoost) and recurrent neural networks (RNNs), to model the temporal and interactive relationships between these parameters. Model performance for predicting preterm pre-eclampsia (<37 weeks) will be compared against the standard FMF multivariate logistic regression algorithm using ROC-AUC and detection rates at fixed false-positive rates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, data-driven tool for superior early identification of women at highest risk for severe, preterm pre-eclampsia. It will demonstrate the translational potential of cross-disciplinary machine learning in obstetrics, potentially enabling more precise allocation of prophylactic interventions like aspirin and personalized surveillance plans.
proposals.novelty: While machine learning has been applied to pre-eclampsia prediction, the direct adaptation of algorithms specifically honed for cardiovascular endothelial dysfunction represents a novel, pathophysiology-informed approach. This moves beyond generic model application to leverage domain-specific algorithmic insights from a related field.

proposals.title: Geospatial Mapping of Pre-Eclampsia Incidence in a Multi-Ethnic London Cohort: Identifying Environmental and Socioeconomic Hotspots for Targeted Prevention
proposals.motivation: Pre-eclampsia remains a leading cause of maternal and perinatal morbidity, with significant variation in incidence suggesting environmental or social determinants. While the ASPRE trial demonstrated effective first-trimester screening and prevention, the underlying population-level risk factors are not fully understood. Geospatial analysis, a cornerstone of modern epidemiology, has been underutilized in maternal-fetal medicine to identify modifiable community-level risks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the incidence of pre-eclampsia clusters in specific geographic hotspots within London, and that these clusters are significantly associated with localized environmental exposures (e.g., air pollution) and area-level socioeconomic deprivation indices, independent of individual patient characteristics.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using anonymized patient data from the Harris Birthright Research Centre's ultrasound databases (2015-2025), linked to postcode. Incidence rates will be calculated and mapped using ArcGIS. We will perform spatial autocorrelation analysis (Global and Local Moran's I) to identify significant clusters (hotspots/coldspots). These clusters will be statistically linked via spatial regression models (e.g., geographically weighted regression) to high-resolution environmental data (PM2.5, NO2 from the London Atmospheric Emissions Inventory) and the Index of Multiple Deprivation (IMD) at the Lower Super Output Area level.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce the first high-resolution geospatial risk map for pre-eclampsia in a major, diverse urban center. It will quantify the independent contribution of place-based environmental and social factors to pre-eclampsia risk. The findings will inform public health policy by identifying high-risk zones for targeted community-level interventions and refined patient-level risk assessment.
proposals.novelty: This applies robust spatial epidemiology methods, common in infectious disease and chronic illness research, to the complex etiology of pre-eclampsia. It moves beyond individual risk factors to model how a woman's residential location, and its associated exposures, modifies her risk, integrating clinical ultrasound data with environmental and social datasets.

proposals.title: Continuous Wearable Monitoring for the Prediction of Preterm Birth in High-Risk Twin Pregnancies: A Prospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Preterm birth, especially in twin pregnancies, remains a leading cause of neonatal morbidity and mortality. Current risk assessment relies on intermittent clinical measurements, which may miss critical, dynamic physiological changes preceding preterm labor. Continuous glucose monitoring in diabetes has revolutionized care; applying similar continuous, non-invasive monitoring to pregnancy could provide unprecedented, real-time data on maternal physiology to identify early warning signs.
proposals.hypothesis: That continuous, multi-parameter wearable sensor data (heart rate variability, skin temperature, and physical activity), analyzed with time-series machine learning models, can identify predictive signatures of impending preterm labor in high-risk twin pregnancies earlier than standard clinical assessments.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study of 200 women with dichorionic twin pregnancies from 20 weeks of gestation. Participants will wear a commercially available, research-grade wearable sensor (e.g., Empatica E4) continuously. Raw physiological signals will be processed to extract daily features (nocturnal heart rate, circadian rhythm metrics, activity-rest cycles). We will develop a recurrent neural network (RNN) architecture, specifically a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) model, to analyze the temporal evolution of these features. The model will be trained to predict spontaneous preterm birth (<34 weeks) using data up to 7 days prior to the event, compared to a control group delivering at term.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal dataset of continuous physiological parameters in twin pregnancies and establish a novel, data-driven predictive model for preterm birth. It will move the field from static risk scoring to dynamic, personalized risk assessment, potentially enabling timely interventions like cervical cerclage or corticosteroid administration.
proposals.novelty: While wearables are used in pregnancy for activity tracking, their application for predictive analytics of preterm birth using deep learning on multi-parameter continuous data in high-risk twins is novel. This approach directly translates a successful paradigm from chronic disease management (diabetes) to obstetrics.

proposals.title: A Clinical Management Protocol for Women with a False-Negative First-Trimester Pre-Eclampsia Screening Result: A Prospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: The Fetal Medicine Foundation's first-trimester combined screening algorithm and the ASPRE trial have established effective early prediction and prevention of preterm preeclampsia. However, a significant clinical gap exists for managing the women who screen as low-risk but subsequently develop preeclampsia or other placental complications. These 'false-negative' cases present late, often with severe disease, due to the absence of enhanced surveillance protocols.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a standardized, second-line surveillance and management protocol for women with a false-negative first-trimester preeclampsia screen will lead to earlier detection of hypertensive disorders and improved perinatal outcomes compared to routine care.
proposals.method: This will be a prospective, multicenter cohort study. Women with a false-negative first-trimester screen (low-risk per ASPRE algorithm) who develop new-onset hypertension after 20 weeks will be enrolled. The intervention protocol includes serial maternal blood pressure monitoring, uterine artery Doppler, fetal biometry, and biochemical markers (PlGF, sFlt-1) at defined gestational intervals (e.g., 24, 28, 32, 36 weeks). Outcomes (gestational age at delivery, birth weight, incidence of severe preeclampsia) will be compared to a historical control cohort of false-negative women managed with routine care, using multivariate regression to adjust for confounders.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first evidence-based protocol for the clinical management of a high-risk group currently falling outside established care pathways. It will translate prediction science into actionable clinical guidance for the entire screened population, thereby improving safety and outcomes for mothers and babies where primary prevention fails.
proposals.novelty: While extensive pre-2026 research focuses on optimizing prediction and preventing preeclampsia in high-risk women, this study uniquely addresses the critical, neglected cohort of screen-negative women who nonetheless develop the disease, turning a screening limitation into a structured care opportunity.

proposals.title: Optimization of a Combined First-Trimester Screening Algorithm for Preterm Preeclampsia: A Risk-Weighted Analysis to Maximize Detection and Minimize False Positives in Singleton and Twin Pregnancies
proposals.motivation: First-trimester combined screening for preterm preeclampsia, using maternal factors, biophysical markers (mean arterial pressure, uterine artery Doppler), and biochemical markers (PlGF), is established but requires optimization. The current fixed-risk cut-off (e.g., 1 in 100) from trials like ASPRE may not represent the optimal clinical or psychological trade-off between detection rate (DR) and false-positive rate (FPR) across different populations, particularly in twin pregnancies where baseline risk is higher and screening performance differs. A systematic analysis to identify the most efficient risk threshold is needed to improve screening efficiency and patient acceptability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a population-specific, risk-weighted optimization of the combined screening algorithm's cut-off will identify a threshold that achieves a superior balance between DR and FPR compared to the current standard, particularly for twin pregnancies where the risk-benefit profile is distinct.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of individual patient data (IPD) from large prospective cohorts, including the ASPRE trial population and twin pregnancy datasets. Using pre-2026 statistical methods, we will perform a decision curve analysis (DCA) to evaluate the net clinical benefit across a continuous range of risk thresholds (from 1 in 2 to 1 in 500). The optimization will aim to maximize the Youden Index and evaluate weighted metrics that penalize false positives. Analyses will be stratified for singleton and twin pregnancies to derive population-specific optimal cut-offs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide evidence-based, optimized risk cut-offs for first-trimester preeclampsia screening tailored to singleton and twin pregnancies. It will directly inform clinical guidelines by quantifying the trade-offs between detection and false alarms, potentially improving the psychological evaluation of screening reported by women. The findings will offer a framework for implementing more efficient, personalized screening protocols.
proposals.novelty: While combined screening is established, this is the first IPD meta-analysis to use decision curve analysis and net benefit frameworks to formally optimize the risk cut-off, moving beyond fixed thresholds. The explicit focus on deriving separate, evidence-based thresholds for twin pregnancies, a high-risk group with distinct screening performance, addresses a significant gap in current practice.

proposals.title: The Added Predictive Value of Ductus Venosus Flow and Megacystis in First-Trimester Combined Screening for Preterm Birth and Adverse Perinatal Outcomes in Twin Pregnancies
proposals.motivation: Preterm birth remains the leading cause of neonatal morbidity and mortality in twin pregnancies. Current first-trimester combined screening, utilizing maternal factors, biometry, and biochemistry, effectively predicts preeclampsia but has limited performance for preterm birth. Pre-2026, sonographic markers like abnormal ductus venosus (DV) flow and fetal megacystis have been individually associated with aneuploidy and adverse outcomes, but their quantitative, combined value in a multi-marker risk algorithm for preterm birth in twins is unknown.
proposals.hypothesis: The integration of first-trimester sonographic markers (specifically abnormal ductus venosus pulsatility index for veins [DV-PIV] and fetal megacystis) into the established combined screening algorithm will significantly improve the prediction of spontaneous preterm birth <34 weeks and a composite of severe adverse perinatal outcomes in dichorionic twin pregnancies.
proposals.method: This will be a prospective cohort study of women with dichorionic twin pregnancies undergoing first-trimester screening at 11-13+6 weeks. Alongside standard screening (nuchal translucency, crown-rump length, serum PAPP-A/β-hCG), dedicated Doppler assessment of DV-PIV and bladder length measurement will be performed. Multivariable logistic regression and machine learning (Random Forest) models will be developed. The primary outcome is spontaneous preterm birth <34 weeks; a secondary composite includes intrauterine death, severe neonatal morbidity, or birth <32 weeks. Model performance will be assessed using area under the ROC curve (AUC), net reclassification improvement (NRI), and decision curve analysis, comparing models with and without the novel sonographic markers.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first evidence-based, quantified estimate of the incremental value of DV flow and megacystis in first-trimester risk stratification for twins. It will deliver a validated, multi-parametric predictive model that could be integrated into clinical care pathways, enabling targeted surveillance and potential early intervention strategies for high-risk twin pregnancies identified in the first trimester.
proposals.novelty: While DV and megacystis have been studied for aneuploidy, their systematic integration into a combined screening algorithm specifically for preterm birth in twins is novel. The application of machine learning to optimize the combination of these continuous and categorical markers for a multi-factorial outcome in a high-risk population represents a significant methodological advance over existing logistic regression-based models.

proposals.title: Quantitative and Qualitative Analysis of Patient Preferences for First-Trimester Pre-Eclampsia Screening: A Mixed-Methods Study Following the ASPRE Trial
proposals.motivation: The ASPRE trial demonstrated the efficacy of first-trimester screening and aspirin prophylaxis for pre-eclampsia. Despite its success, the underlying drivers of its high patient acceptance, as noted in qualitative evaluations, are not formally quantified or fully understood. A systematic investigation of patient preferences is crucial to optimize future screening program design, improve shared decision-making, and ensure high uptake in diverse populations, including those at high risk for preterm birth such as twin pregnancies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the preference for first-trimester screening is primarily driven by a combination of psychological factors (early reassurance, perceived control) and practical considerations (timing for intervention, integration with routine nuchal translucency scan), rather than by the statistical performance of the test alone.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, mixed-methods study in a large London fetal medicine unit. A validated discrete choice experiment (DCE) will be designed using attributes like timing of screening, detection rate, false-positive rate, and type of preventive intervention. This will be administered to 500 pregnant women before their first-trimester scan to quantify preference weights. Concurrently, in-depth semi-structured interviews (n=30-40) will explore the reasoning behind choices, focusing on themes of risk perception, anxiety, and the value of early knowledge. Data analysis will involve hierarchical Bayesian modeling for the DCE and thematic analysis using the Framework Method for interview transcripts.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first robust, quantitative model of patient utilities for pre-eclampsia screening timing and characteristics. It will generate a validated preference instrument for future health economic evaluations and offer deep qualitative insights to guide patient-centered counseling. The findings will directly inform clinical guidelines on how to present screening options to enhance patient engagement and adherence.
proposals.novelty: While the ASPRE trial evaluated women's experiences, this study formally applies health preference research methods (DCE) to pre-eclampsia screening, moving from descriptive evaluation to predictive modeling of choice. It uniquely integrates this quantitative model with rich qualitative data to explain the 'why' behind the preference weights, a novel approach in this clinical domain.

proposals.title: A Machine Learning Framework for the Differential Prediction of Specific Chromosomal Abnormalities from First-Trimester Combined Screening Marker Patterns
proposals.motivation: First-trimester combined screening (FTS) effectively identifies pregnancies at high risk for common aneuploidies like trisomy 21, but the pattern of biochemical (PAPP-A, β-hCG) and sonographic (nuchal translucency) markers can also suggest other, rarer chromosomal abnormalities. Current risk algorithms are optimized for a binary (high/low) outcome for a single condition, lacking the granularity to differentiate between specific abnormalities, which is crucial for guiding appropriate diagnostic testing and counseling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that unique, multivariate patterns of first-trimester screening markers are associated with specific chromosomal abnormalities beyond trisomy 21, and that machine learning algorithms can be trained to perform this differential prediction with clinically useful accuracy.
proposals.method: We will utilize a large, multi-center database of FTS results linked to definitive karyotype outcomes. The dataset will include cases of trisomies 21, 18, and 13, sex chromosome aneuploidies (e.g., Turner syndrome), triploidy, and other rare abnormalities. We will develop and compare several algorithms: 1) a multiclass gradient boosting model (XGBoost) using the raw marker values and maternal demographics, 2) a multinomial logistic regression model with engineered interaction terms, and 3) a feed-forward neural network. Model performance will be evaluated using multiclass metrics (e.g., F1-score per class) on a held-out test set.
proposals.contribution: This work will move prenatal screening from a binary risk assessment for a single condition towards a more nuanced, multi-class diagnostic aid. It will provide a proof-of-concept tool that could refine patient counseling by suggesting the most likely underlying abnormality in high-risk cases, potentially informing the choice of confirmatory test (e.g., CVS vs. specific microarray analysis).
proposals.novelty: While FTS markers are known to have different distributions across abnormalities, existing models do not attempt to classify the specific abnormality. This proposal frames the challenge as an inverse problem and applies modern, multi-class machine learning techniques explicitly designed for differential diagnosis within the prenatal screening context.

proposals.title: Temporal Stability of the Observed-to-Expected Lung-to-Head Ratio (O/E LHR) in Predicting Survival in Fetal Congenital Diaphragmatic Hernia: A Multi-Era Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: The O/E LHR is a key prenatal prognostic marker for survival in fetal congenital diaphragmatic hernia (CDH). However, postnatal management, including ventilation strategies and the availability of ECMO, has evolved significantly over the past two decades. It is unknown whether the predictive performance of O/E LHR, typically derived from historical cohorts, remains stable and generalizable across different treatment eras, which is critical for its use in contemporary counseling and risk stratification.
proposals.hypothesis: The predictive accuracy of the O/E LHR for neonatal survival in isolated CDH is independent of the postnatal treatment era, despite major advances in neonatal intensive care protocols.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using data from the Fetal Medicine Foundation and collaborating centers, spanning from 2000 to 2025. Cohorts will be stratified into distinct treatment eras (e.g., 2000-2009, 2010-2019, 2020-2025) based on major shifts in management guidelines. The primary outcome is neonatal survival to discharge. We will assess the temporal stability of O/E LHR by constructing era-specific logistic regression models and comparing their discrimination (using area under the ROC curve, AUC) and calibration. The primary analysis will test for non-inferiority in AUC between the earliest and most recent eras.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide crucial evidence on the temporal validity of a cornerstone prenatal biomarker. It will either confirm its robustness for contemporary use or demonstrate the need for era-specific recalibration, directly impacting prenatal counseling accuracy. The findings will establish a framework for evaluating other prenatal prediction models in the context of evolving postnatal care.
proposals.novelty: While O/E LHR is widely used, its performance has not been formally tested for invariance across major shifts in neonatal care. This study moves beyond validating the marker in a single cohort to explicitly testing its stability as a *treatment-independent* prognostic tool, a novel and clinically essential question.

proposals.title: Prospective Multicenter Validation of the Observed-to-Expected Lung-to-Head Ratio (O/E LHR) Model for Predicting Survival in Fetuses with Congenital Diaphragmatic Hernia
proposals.motivation: Congenital diaphragmatic hernia (CDH) remains a major cause of neonatal mortality and morbidity. Accurate prenatal prediction of survival is crucial for parental counseling and clinical management, including decisions regarding fetal therapy and delivery planning. Existing predictive models, primarily based on retrospective data for the Observed-to-Expected Lung-to-Head Ratio (O/E LHR), side of the defect, and gestational age, require robust prospective validation in a diverse, multicenter population to confirm their generalizability and clinical utility.
proposals.hypothesis: The prenatal predictive model incorporating O/E LHR, side of the diaphragmatic defect (left vs. right), and gestational age at measurement will demonstrate high accuracy (AUC >0.85) for predicting neonatal survival to discharge when prospectively validated in an independent, multicenter cohort.
proposals.method: This will be a prospective, observational cohort study conducted across 15-20 international fetal medicine centers. Pregnancies diagnosed with isolated CDH between 18-32 weeks of gestation will be included. Standardized ultrasound protocols will be used to measure O/E LHR. The primary outcome is survival to hospital discharge. Predictive performance will be assessed by calculating the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC), calibration plots, and decision curve analysis, comparing the model's predictions against observed outcomes. Statistical analysis will follow TRIPOD guidelines for prediction model validation.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level, prospective, multicenter evidence for the clinical validity of the O/E LHR-based model. It will establish definitive, generalizable performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, predictive values) to guide clinicians in risk stratification. The results will form the evidence base for standardized prenatal counseling protocols and inform the selection of candidates for fetal endoscopic tracheal occlusion (FETO).
proposals.novelty: While O/E LHR is widely used, its predictive model has not undergone rigorous prospective validation across multiple centers with standardized protocols, which is a critical step before widespread implementation as a clinical decision-making tool. This study moves beyond retrospective derivation and single-center series to provide Level II evidence for this key prenatal prognostic tool.

proposals.title: Long-term Pulmonary and Neurodevelopmental Outcomes in Survivors of Congenital Diaphragmatic Hernia: A Cohort Study Stratified by Antenatal Observed-to-Expected Lung-to-Head Ratio (O/E LHR)
proposals.motivation: Congenital diaphragmatic hernia (CDH) remains a major cause of neonatal mortality, with antenatal O/E LHR a key prognostic marker for postnatal survival. However, pre-2026 research, including work from Professor Nicolaides' group, has focused predominantly on survival as the primary outcome. For survivors, significant long-term morbidity, including chronic lung disease and neurodevelopmental delay, is a major concern, yet the predictive value of O/E LHR for these long-term outcomes is poorly defined.
proposals.hypothesis: In survivors of isolated CDH, the antenatal O/E LHR, measured between 18-32 weeks of gestation, is inversely correlated with the severity of long-term pulmonary morbidity and neurodevelopmental impairment at 2 and 5 years of corrected age.
proposals.method: This will be a retrospective cohort study of CDH survivors from a pre-existing multicentre database (2008-2025). The primary exposure is the antenatal O/E LHR, categorized per established pre-2026 thresholds (<25%, 25-45%, >45%). Primary outcomes are: 1) Pulmonary morbidity (assessed via need for home oxygen at discharge, re-hospitalizations, and standardized pulmonary function testing at age 5), and 2) Neurodevelopmental outcome (assessed using the Bayley Scales of Infant and Toddler Development, 3rd or 4th edition, at 2 years). Statistical analysis will employ multivariable logistic and linear regression, adjusting for known confounders such as gestational age at birth, patch repair, and duration of neonatal hospitalization.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive evidence linking the widely used antenatal prognostic marker, O/E LHR, to long-term functional outcomes in CDH survivors. It will refine prenatal counselling by quantifying risks beyond survival and identify a high-risk subgroup of survivors who may benefit from targeted long-term multidisciplinary follow-up and early intervention programs.
proposals.novelty: While O/E LHR is established for predicting mortality, its application as a specific predictor of long-term pulmonary and neurodevelopmental morbidity in survivors is novel and moves the field beyond the binary outcome of survival versus death.

proposals.title: Prognostic Validation of the Observed-to-Expected Lung-to-Head Ratio (O/E LHR) in Fetuses with Congenital Diaphragmatic Hernia and Associated Structural Anomalies: A Multicenter Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: The observed-to-expected lung-to-head ratio (O/E LHR) is a key prenatal prognostic marker for isolated congenital diaphragmatic hernia (CDH), guiding parental counseling and perinatal management. However, up to 40% of CDH cases have associated congenital anomalies, which are a major determinant of poor outcome. The prognostic performance of O/E LHR in this complex, high-risk subgroup remains unvalidated, creating significant uncertainty in risk stratification and timing-of-birth decisions for these pregnancies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the O/E LHR retains independent prognostic value for neonatal survival and need for extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO) in fetuses with non-isolated CDH, but that its predictive accuracy is modified by the type and severity of associated anomalies.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective, multicenter cohort study of fetuses with a prenatal diagnosis of CDH and at least one major extra-diaphragmatic anomaly, diagnosed between 18 and 32 weeks of gestation from 2010-2025. Standardized O/E LHR measurement from stored ultrasound images will be performed centrally. The primary outcome is survival to discharge. We will use multivariable logistic regression to assess the independent association of O/E LHR with survival, adjusting for anomaly type (using a standardized classification), gestational age at diagnosis, and side of hernia. Receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analysis will determine the predictive accuracy of O/E LHR and be compared to models for isolated CDH.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first robust, evidence-based validation of O/E LHR in non-isolated CDH, directly informing prenatal counseling with tailored survival probabilities. It will clarify whether established O/E LHR cut-offs for isolated CDH can be applied to complex cases, thereby refining risk-based antenatal management and resource planning for neonatal surgery and ECMO.
proposals.novelty: While O/E LHR is well-studied in isolated CDH, its utility in the context of concurrent anomalies—a common and critical clinical scenario—is entirely unexplored. This study moves beyond the 'isolated' paradigm to address a major gap in fetal medicine practice.

proposals.title: Development of a Standardized Prenatal Risk Stratification Tool for Congenital Diaphragmatic Hernia Using Gestational Age-Adjusted Lung Size Z-Scores
proposals.motivation: Congenital diaphragmatic hernia (CDH) carries high neonatal mortality, primarily due to pulmonary hypoplasia. Current prenatal assessment, using metrics like the observed-to-expected lung-to-head ratio (O/E LHR), lacks standardization across gestational ages, complicating risk prediction and counseling. A standardized, gestational-age-adjusted tool is urgently needed to improve prognostic accuracy and guide management decisions, analogous to the role of Z-scores in fetal cardiology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting the Z-score methodology from fetal echocardiography to create gestational age-adjusted Z-scores for fetal lung size measurements (e.g., LHR, total lung volume) will provide a more accurate and standardized tool for prenatal risk stratification in CDH, correlating strongly with postnatal survival and need for extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO).
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of fetuses with isolated CDH from 18-38 weeks' gestation. Using stored ultrasound/MRI images, we will measure lung size parameters. We will establish reference ranges (mean, standard deviation) for these parameters across gestation in a large control population. For the CDH cohort, we will calculate individual Z-scores [(observed - mean for GA)/SD for GA]. We will then perform multivariable logistic regression to compare the predictive performance of Z-scores against traditional O/E LHR for outcomes of survival and ECMO use, using receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first validated, gestational age-adjusted Z-score model for fetal lung size in CDH, creating a universal 'language' for severity. It will directly contribute to more precise prenatal counseling and risk-adapted management protocols. The methodology could be integrated into clinical software, standardizing assessment globally.
proposals.novelty: While Z-scores are standard in fetal echocardiography, their application to lung morphometry for CDH is novel. This approach moves beyond simple ratios to a statistically robust, GA-adjusted metric, addressing a key limitation in existing prenatal prediction models.

proposals.title: Dose-Dependent Effects of Aspirin on Trophoblast Invasion, Syncytialization, and Inflammatory Cytokine Secretion: An In Vitro Mechanistic Study
proposals.motivation: Low-dose aspirin (LDA) is a key intervention for preventing preeclampsia, particularly in high-risk women identified by first-trimester screening (e.g., ASPRE trial). However, its precise cellular mechanism of action on the placenta remains incompletely understood. Furthermore, the optimal dose for maximizing placental benefit while minimizing side effects, especially in complex pregnancies like twins which have a higher baseline risk of preterm birth and preeclampsia, is not fully defined at the cellular level.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that aspirin exerts a dose-dependent, biphasic effect on primary human trophoblast function, where a low dose (e.g., 100-150mg) optimally enhances invasion and dampens inflammation, while higher doses may impair these critical processes. We further hypothesize that trophoblasts from twin pregnancies will exhibit a differential response profile compared to singletons.
proposals.method: Primary human cytotrophoblasts will be isolated from term placentas (singleton and dichorionic twin pregnancies) and cultured. Cells will be treated with a range of aspirin concentrations (50µM to 1000µM, reflecting low to high dose) for 24-72 hours. Functional assays will quantify invasion (Matrigel transwell), syncytialization (β-hCG secretion, E-cadherin immunofluorescence), and secretion of key inflammatory mediators (IL-6, TNF-α, sFlt-1, PlGF) via multiplex ELISA. Statistical analysis will employ mixed-effects models to compare dose-response curves between singleton and twin-derived trophoblasts, with post-hoc testing for specific concentration effects.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, dose-response characterization of aspirin's direct effects on primary human trophoblast biology. It will clarify the cellular rationale for the 150mg dose used in prevention and may identify a therapeutic window. By comparing singleton and twin trophoblasts, it will offer mechanistic insight into the differential efficacy of aspirin in twin pregnancies, informing future clinical trial design.
proposals.novelty: While aspirin's clinical effect is known, a systematic in vitro dose-response analysis linking specific concentrations to distinct trophoblast functional endpoints is lacking. Directly comparing the cellular pharmacodynamics in trophoblasts from singleton versus twin pregnancies is a novel approach to understanding variable clinical responses.

proposals.title: A Biomarker and Uterine Artery Doppler-Based Risk Model for Preterm Birth and Preeclampsia in Twin Pregnancies: Minimizing Reliance on Maternal Age
proposals.motivation: Current first-trimester screening models for adverse outcomes like preterm birth and preeclampsia in twin pregnancies heavily weight maternal age, a non-modifiable factor. This can introduce systematic bias, potentially misclassifying risk for younger women with other significant risk factors and older women with otherwise low-risk profiles. Developing a model that minimizes this reliance is crucial for equitable, personalized antenatal care focused on modifiable pathophysiology.
proposals.hypothesis: A multivariable risk prediction model for preterm birth (<34 weeks) and preterm preeclampsia in twin pregnancies, constructed primarily from first-trimester biomarkers (PAPP-A, PlGF), mean uterine artery pulsatility index (UtA-PI), and detailed obstetric history, will achieve non-inferior performance to an age-dependent model while significantly reducing age-based screening bias.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of prospectively collected data from a large cohort of twin pregnancies (n~2000) with first-trimester measurements. Using logistic regression and machine learning techniques (e.g., Random Forest), we will develop a model where maternal age is deliberately down-weighted or entered last. Model performance (AUC, detection rate at 10% FPR) will be compared against a standard model with age as a primary factor. Internal validation will use bootstrapping, and calibration will be assessed.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, clinically applicable screening protocol for twins that focuses on pathophysiological markers rather than chronological age. It will directly inform the design of future intervention trials (like timed birth or aspirin prophylaxis) by identifying high-risk patients based on biomarker profiles, not age demographics. The methodology will set a precedent for reducing demographic bias in obstetric risk assessment.
proposals.novelty: While the ASPRE trial model for preeclampsia in singletons uses biomarkers and UtA-PI, its application in twins remains debated, and age is a key component. This proposal specifically seeks to deconstruct the age-dependency of risk in the complex twin gestation context, creating a more biologically grounded screening tool, which is a novel and necessary advancement.

proposals.title: Mapping Parental Values in Prenatal Screening: A Participatory Design Study to Develop an Adaptive Decision-Support Framework for First-Trimester Risk Assessment
proposals.motivation: Current prenatal screening, including first-trimester pre-eclampsia and aneuploidy screening, is framed around a singular medical goal of risk detection and prevention. This monolithic approach fails to account for the heterogeneous values, intentions, and decisional needs of prospective parents, potentially undermining reproductive autonomy. As screening becomes more complex and widespread, there is an urgent need to align clinical tools with a pluralistic model of parental goals, particularly in diverse populations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct, measurable parental archetypes exist based on their values, informational preferences, and decisional goals regarding prenatal screening, and that these archetypes can be used to algorithmically tailor decision-support tools to improve alignment with personal autonomy and reduce decisional conflict.
proposals.method: We will conduct a mixed-methods, participatory design study with a demographically diverse cohort of pregnant women and their partners. First, qualitative interviews and value-elicitation workshops will identify key value dimensions. Second, a large-scale survey will quantify these dimensions, using latent class analysis to define distinct user archetypes. Third, we will develop a prototype adaptive platform using a rule-based algorithmic engine that maps user-stated preferences (collected via a pre-test questionnaire) to tailored information dashboards, counseling resource recommendations, and follow-up option pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical taxonomy of parental values in contemporary prenatal screening, moving beyond theoretical ethics. It will deliver a functional prototype for value-sensitive clinical decision support, offering a practical model to operationalize personalized, autonomy-respecting care in high-volume screening pathways like the Fetal Medicine Foundation's first-trimester combined test.
proposals.novelty: While prior work evaluates screening acceptance or psychological impact, this study proactively maps the full spectrum of parental goals to *design* adaptive tools. It integrates participatory design and latent class analysis from social science directly into the clinical informatics of prenatal care, a novel translational approach.

proposals.title: The Prenatal-Postnatal Continuum: A Randomized Trial of a Structured Care Pathway Following First-Trimester Screening for Preterm Birth and Preeclampsia in Twin Pregnancies
proposals.motivation: Current prenatal screening, particularly for conditions like preterm birth and preeclampsia in twins, often culminates in a binary decision regarding invasive testing or termination, leaving families unprepared for postnatal realities. This creates a significant care gap, as screen-positive pregnancies are at high risk for adverse neonatal and childhood outcomes requiring specialized support. A paradigm shift is needed to leverage screening not as an endpoint, but as the initiation of a proactive, longitudinal management plan.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a standardized, multidisciplinary care pathway triggered by a first-trimester screen-positive result for preterm birth/preeclampsia in twin pregnancies will improve parental preparedness, postnatal service engagement, and early childhood developmental outcomes at 24 months, compared to standard care.
proposals.method: We will conduct a two-arm randomized controlled trial in women with twin pregnancies. The intervention arm will receive a structured pathway following a screen-positive result at 11-13 weeks, involving immediate consultation with a dedicated coordinator, creation of a personalized postnatal plan with neonatology and pediatric specialists, and facilitated links to early intervention services. The control arm will receive standard care. Primary outcomes will be parental preparedness (validated questionnaire) and Bayley Scales of Infant Development scores at 24 months corrected age. Analysis will use intention-to-treat with mixed-effects models to account for gestational age at birth.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first high-level evidence for a integrated prenatal-postnatal care model in high-risk obstetrics. It will generate a reproducible framework for transforming screening into a platform for long-term family support, potentially improving child neurodevelopment and parental well-being. The findings will directly inform clinical guidelines for the management of screen-positive twin pregnancies.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond the established ASPRE trial's focus on prevention, by applying the 'screening-as-pathway-initiation' concept from oncology to prenatal care, creating a tangible bridge between obstetric risk identification and pediatric follow-up that is currently absent.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Continuous Fetal Health Index from Prenatal Biomarkers: A Longitudinal Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Current prenatal screening is largely binary, focusing on specific disease risks (e.g., aneuploidy, preeclampsia) and often creating anxiety. There is a critical unmet need to provide expectant parents with continuous, positive information about general fetal well-being and developmental progress. This is especially pertinent for high-risk pregnancies, such as twins, where monitoring for preterm birth and other complications is intensive but fragmented.
proposals.hypothesis: A composite index, derived from serial measurements of established and emerging prenatal biomarkers, can be computationally modeled to provide a continuous, dynamic signal of general fetal physiological status and resilience, correlating with key pregnancy outcomes like gestational age at delivery and birth weight.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal cohort study of 1000 singleton and twin pregnancies from 11-13 weeks to delivery. Serial plasma samples will be analyzed for established biomarkers (PAPP-A, PlGF, AFP, hCG) and novel candidates (e.g., cell-free RNA profiles). We will employ functional data analysis and mixed-effects modeling to create individual temporal trajectories. A machine learning architecture, specifically a recurrent neural network (RNN), will integrate these trajectories with ultrasound biometry to generate a weekly 'Fetal Health Index' (FHI), validated against outcomes including preterm birth, fetal growth restriction, and preeclampsia.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative framework for holistic fetal assessment, moving beyond isolated risk scores. It will offer clinicians a tool for monitoring general well-being and potentially identifying early, sub-clinical signs of physiological stress. For parents, it translates complex biomarker data into an understandable 'dashboard' of fetal health.
proposals.novelty: The innovation lies in re-purposing and integrating existing prenatal biomarkers not for single-disease prediction, but for the continuous modeling of general physiological state—a shift from diagnosis to wellness monitoring. The application of functional data analysis and RNNs to model this longitudinal biomarker 'phenome' in pregnancy is novel.

proposals.title: A Multi-omics Constellation Analysis of Maternal Plasma for the Early Detection of Fetal Growth Restriction and Hypoxic Stress
proposals.motivation: Current prenatal screening excels at detecting aneuploidies but offers limited insight into the general physiologic well-being of the fetus. Conditions like fetal growth restriction (FGR) and chronic hypoxia are major contributors to stillbirth, preterm birth, and long-term neurodevelopmental morbidity, yet they often remain undetected until late gestation. There is a critical need for a non-invasive, early-pregnancy screening tool that can assess overall fetal health, analogous to the comprehensive risk assessment pioneered for preeclampsia.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a constellation of subtle, correlated deviations in maternal plasma multi-omics profiles—including cell-free RNA, methylation, and proteomics—from a healthy normative baseline provides a quantifiable signature of fetal physiologic stress, enabling the early identification of pregnancies at high risk for FGR and hypoxic complications.
proposals.method: We will perform a nested case-control study within a large prospective cohort, analyzing banked maternal plasma samples from 11-13 weeks' gestation. Cases will be pregnancies with subsequent diagnosis of FGR or adverse outcomes indicative of hypoxia. Controls will be uncomplicated term deliveries. Multi-omics data will be integrated, and a normative baseline will be established from the control group. We will employ a multi-step analytical pipeline using dimensionality reduction (e.g., variational autoencoders) followed by a supervised classifier (e.g., XGBoost) trained to detect the coordinated deviation patterns, or 'constellation', associated with adverse fetal physiology.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, integrative biomarker model for early fetal health assessment, moving beyond single-disease detection. It will provide a foundational framework for a 'fetal health index' that could be integrated into first-trimester combined screening. The methodology establishes a new paradigm for inferring the state of an unseen system (the fetus) through high-dimensional proxy data (maternal multi-omics).
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its conceptual inversion from detecting specific anomalies to inferring general physiologic state, and in its application of an integrated, pattern-based 'constellation' analysis to prenatal multi-omics data, a method not previously applied for this purpose.

proposals.title: A Multi-Marker First-Trimester Screening Algorithm for the Prediction of Adverse Perinatal Outcomes in Twin Pregnancies: A Prospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Current first-trimester screening in twins primarily focuses on aneuploidy and preeclampsia (as per the ASPRE model). However, twins are at significantly increased risk of adverse perinatal outcomes like preterm birth, growth restriction, and stillbirth, which are not directly targeted by existing early screening paradigms. A comprehensive, early marker of general fetal well-being is critically needed to stratify risk and guide antenatal surveillance in this high-risk population.
proposals.hypothesis: Deviations from established normative values of combined biochemical (PAPP-A, free β-hCG) and biophysical (crown-rump length, nuchal translucency, ductus venosus flow) markers in the first trimester can be integrated into a single, continuous risk score that predicts a composite endpoint of severe adverse perinatal outcome (SAPO) in twin pregnancies, independent of chorionicity.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study of women with twin pregnancies undergoing first-trimester screening at 11-13+6 weeks. Using individual patient-level data, we will develop a multivariate logistic regression model. The model will incorporate established markers (adjusted for chorionicity) and derive a 'Fetal Health Index' (FHI). Machine learning techniques (e.g., random forest) will be used to validate the model and identify optimal risk cut-offs. The primary outcome is SAPO, defined as intrauterine death, birth <32 weeks, or birth weight <3rd percentile.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first validated, early-pregnancy tool for the holistic assessment of fetal health risk in twins, moving beyond disease-specific screening. It will enable the identification of a high-risk subgroup for intensified monitoring and potential early intervention studies, and a low-risk group for reassurance, optimizing resource allocation.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the traditional screening paradigm by using the panel of first-trimester markers not to rule out a specific condition, but to rule in general fetal vulnerability. It synthesizes pre-2026 knowledge on twin-specific marker norms and adverse outcome prediction into a single, novel clinical instrument.

proposals.title: A Dynamic, Marker-Specific Risk Threshold Framework for First-Trimester Combined Screening: Optimizing the Balance Between Detection and Invasive Testing Rates
proposals.motivation: Current first-trimester combined screening for aneuploidies and preeclampsia relies on fixed risk thresholds (e.g., 1 in 100) to recommend invasive testing, a one-size-fits-all approach. This fails to account for the specific pattern of biomarker deviations (e.g., extreme PAPP-A with normal PlGF), which may indicate different underlying risks. A more nuanced, personalized threshold framework is needed to improve clinical utility and ethical acceptability by better tailoring the invasive testing decision to an individual's unique biochemical profile.
proposals.hypothesis: The optimal risk threshold for recommending invasive diagnostic testing (CVS/amniocentesis) is not fixed but varies dynamically based on the specific combination and magnitude of deviations in the first-trimester biochemical marker profile.
proposals.method: We will analyze individual patient data from large prospective cohorts (e.g., ASPRE trial participants) with first-trimester biomarker results (PAPP-A, β-hCG, PlGF, others) and known pregnancy outcomes. Using machine learning techniques, specifically gradient boosting machines (e.g., XGBoost) and interpretable rule-based models, we will develop a framework that maps multi-analyte signatures to a personalized 'action threshold.' This model will be trained to optimize a clinical utility function that penalizes both missed diagnoses and unnecessary invasive procedures, moving beyond a single risk cutoff.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first evidence-based, adaptive decision framework for prenatal screening, moving clinical practice beyond static risk thresholds. It will offer a practical tool to personalize counseling, potentially increasing the detection rate for serious conditions while reducing the false-positive rate and the associated anxiety and procedure-related losses.
proposals.novelty: While existing research optimizes fixed thresholds, this study innovates by making the threshold itself a conditional output of a multi-marker model, creating a dynamic and personalized decision rule. This shifts the paradigm from finding a single best population cutoff to developing an adaptive, profile-specific interpretation system.

proposals.title: Enhancing First-Trimester Preterm Birth and Preeclampsia Prediction in Singleton and Twin Pregnancies: A Multi-Marker Serum Proteomics and Metabolomics Study
proposals.motivation: The first-trimester combined screening algorithm for preeclampsia, validated in the ASPRE trial, and models for preterm birth risk, primarily rely on maternal factors, biophysical markers (MAP, UtA-PI), and serum PlGF. Despite its utility, the false-positive rate remains a concern, causing unnecessary anxiety and prophylactic interventions. Incorporating novel biochemical analytes could refine risk stratification, particularly for high-risk groups like twin pregnancies, where prediction models are less robust.
proposals.hypothesis: The addition of novel maternal serum proteomic and metabolomic markers, measured at 11-13 weeks' gestation, will significantly improve the detection rate and reduce the false-positive rate of the existing first-trimester combined screening model for preterm preeclampsia and spontaneous preterm birth <34 weeks.
proposals.method: This will be a nested case-control study within a large prospective cohort of singleton and twin pregnancies. Using stored first-trimester serum samples, we will perform targeted mass spectrometry to quantify a pre-defined panel of candidate proteins (e.g., PAPP-A isoforms, other placental proteins like PAPPA2, Glypican-3) and metabolites (e.g., amino acids, carnitines) implicated in placental dysfunction.
