Multivariable logistic regression analysis, adjusting for maternal factors, MAP, UtA-PI, and PlGF, will be used to identify independent markers. Machine learning (LASSO regression for feature selection, followed by Random Forest for model building) will be employed to develop and validate an integrated risk prediction algorithm.
proposals.contribution: This research will generate a validated, enhanced first-trimester screening algorithm with superior performance metrics. It will provide crucial pathophysiological insights into the early biochemical signatures of placental disorders leading to preterm birth and preeclampsia. The findings could directly inform clinical practice by enabling more precise, personalized risk assessment and targeted prevention strategies.
proposals.novelty: While previous work has focused on adding single markers (e.g., PlGF) to biophysical parameters, this study systematically integrates a multi-omics panel of novel serum analytes into the established combined test. It uniquely applies this advanced biochemical profiling to both singleton and twin pregnancies, addressing a significant gap in risk prediction for multiples.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Assessment of Parental Anxiety and Psychological Well-being Following First-Trimester Pre-eclampsia and Preterm Birth Risk Screening in Twin Pregnancies
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, first-trimester combined screening for preeclampsia (as in the ASPRE trial) and preterm birth risk in twins is becoming standard. While clinical outcomes are well-studied, the psychological impact on parents throughout the prolonged, high-risk pregnancy journey—marked by frequent monitoring and complex decisions like timed birth or cerclage—is poorly understood. This gap is critical as parental anxiety can affect pregnancy health and postnatal bonding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that parents of twin pregnancies identified as high-risk for preeclampsia or preterm birth will experience significantly higher levels of anxiety and decisional conflict throughout gestation compared to low-risk parents, but that structured, multidisciplinary counseling will mitigate these adverse psychological outcomes.
proposals.method: This will be a prospective, longitudinal cohort study embedded within a tertiary fetal medicine unit. Women with twin pregnancies and their partners will be recruited at the 11-13 week scan. Psychological assessment using validated tools (e.g., State-Trait Anxiety Inventory, Decisional Conflict Scale, Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale) will be conducted at five timepoints: after first-trimester screening results, at 24-26 weeks (peri-cervical length assessment), 32 weeks, term, and 3 months postpartum. We will use mixed-effects regression models to analyze trajectories of anxiety, controlling for clinical outcomes (e.g., actual preterm birth, preeclampsia) and intervention (e.g., cerclage, aspirin). Qualitative interviews will explore the decision-making experience.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal map of psychological morbidity associated with modern multi-risk screening in twin pregnancies. It will identify critical timepoints of peak distress to target supportive interventions. The findings will directly inform the development of integrated psychological care pathways within fetal medicine, moving beyond a purely biomedical model to improve holistic patient outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While the ASPRE trial evaluated women's initial evaluation of screening, this study uniquely tracks both parents' psychological journey across the entire gestation of high-risk twins, linking specific clinical decision nodes (e.g., cerclage at 24-26 weeks) to mental health outcomes, an approach not previously undertaken.

proposals.title: Optimizing the Risk Cutoff for Invasive Prenatal Testing in Twin Pregnancies: A Decision-Analytic and Clinical Outcome Study
proposals.motivation: In twin pregnancies, the risk-benefit calculus for invasive diagnostic procedures like amniocentesis is complex due to a higher background risk of pregnancy loss compared to singletons. Current risk thresholds for offering testing, often extrapolated from singleton data (e.g., 1 in 150 or 1 in 250), may not be clinically optimal for twins, leading to either unnecessary procedure-related losses or missed diagnoses. Determining a threshold that balances the ethical imperative of accurate diagnosis against the procedural risk is a critical, unresolved issue in prenatal care for multiple pregnancies.
proposals.hypothesis: A risk cutoff higher than the standard 1 in 150 used for singletons will optimize clinical utility and ethical acceptability for invasive testing in twin pregnancies, minimizing total adverse outcomes (procedure-related loss plus live birth with an undiagnosed major condition).
proposals.method: We will conduct a decision-analytic modeling study using a pre-2026 established cohort of dichorionic twin pregnancies with available first-trimester combined screening results (nuchal translucency, biochemistry) and pregnancy outcomes. Using this data, we will perform a Monte Carlo simulation to model outcomes across a spectrum of risk cutoffs (from 1 in 50 to 1 in 500). The model will incorporate known parameters: procedure-related loss rates for twins, prevalence of major aneuploidies, and test performance characteristics. The primary outcome measure will be the 'number needed to harm' (procedure-related loss per diagnosis) balanced against the 'number of missed diagnoses' for each cutoff.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first evidence-based, twin-specific risk threshold for invasive prenatal testing, directly informing clinical guidelines. It will offer a quantitative framework for counseling women with twin pregnancies, moving beyond singleton-derived norms. The study will also establish a methodology for integrating procedural risk, screening performance, and patient values in complex obstetric decision-making.
proposals.novelty: While risk cutoffs have been debated for singletons, this study specifically addresses the unique context of twin pregnancies, where procedural risk is higher and screening performance differs. The application of formal decision-analytic modeling to optimize this threshold for twins, rather than applying a consensus-derived figure, is a novel and necessary approach.

proposals.title: A Portfolio Optimization Approach to Determining the Efficient Frontier of First-Trimester Screening Strategies for Preterm Preeclampsia
proposals.motivation: First-trimester combined screening for preterm preeclampsia, as validated in the ASPRE trial, is a major advance. However, healthcare systems face constrained budgets and must decide how to optimally allocate resources across different screening and prevention pathways. Traditional cost-effectiveness analyses often compare discrete strategies but lack a framework to systematically explore the continuum of possible test combinations and thresholds to find the optimal trade-off between cost and health benefit.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling prenatal screening strategies as a portfolio of health investments with defined costs, detection rates (sensitivity/specificity), and downstream outcomes, modern portfolio optimization techniques can identify an 'efficient frontier' of screening strategies that maximizes health gains for a given resource expenditure or minimizes cost for a required performance level.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Markowitz mean-variance optimization framework. Screening strategies (e.g., combinations of biomarkers, ultrasound, maternal factors at 11-13 weeks) will be modeled as 'assets.' Their 'return' will be the net monetary benefit or QALYs gained, and 'risk' will be the variance in outcome (e.g., false positives, missed cases). Constraints will include total budget, test availability, and performance thresholds. The optimization algorithm will solve for the weighting of each strategy in the optimal portfolio, using data from the ASPRE trial and contemporary UK NHS cost structures. Sensitivity analysis will test the robustness of the efficient frontier.
proposals.contribution: This will provide a novel, quantitative decision-support tool for policymakers to identify the most cost-effective implementation of first-trimester preeclampsia screening. It will move beyond pairwise comparisons to define the optimal mix of screening intensity and resource allocation, directly linking financial constraints to population health outcomes in prenatal care.
proposals.novelty: This is the first application of financial portfolio theory to the optimization of first-trimester screening portfolios, introducing a continuous optimization paradigm to a field dominated by discrete strategy comparisons. It formally treats budget as a central constraint in the screening design process.

proposals.title: A Cost-Effectiveness Analysis of First-Trimester Combined Screening for Preterm Preeclampsia and Spontaneous Preterm Birth in Twin Pregnancies
proposals.motivation: Preterm birth and preeclampsia are leading causes of perinatal morbidity and mortality, with twin pregnancies at significantly elevated risk. The ASPRE trial demonstrated the clinical efficacy of first-trimester combined screening and aspirin prophylaxis for preeclampsia, and emerging models also target spontaneous preterm birth risk. However, the economic implications of implementing such comprehensive first-trimester screening programs in high-risk twin pregnancies, compared to standard care, remain unquantified, posing a barrier to widespread adoption.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a first-trimester combined screening program for preterm preeclampsia and spontaneous preterm birth in twin pregnancies, followed by targeted interventions (e.g., aspirin, cervical length surveillance, cerclage), will be cost-effective compared to existing standard screening methods from a healthcare payer perspective.
proposals.method: We will develop a decision-analytic Markov model comparing the proposed screening strategy to standard care. The model will simulate a cohort of women with twin pregnancies from the first trimester through the lifetime of the mother and offspring. Clinical probabilities (e.g., detection rates, intervention efficacy from trials like ASPRE and cerclage meta-analyses) and costs will be derived from published literature and UK NHS reference costs. Health outcomes will be measured in quality-adjusted life-years (QALYs). We will perform deterministic and probabilistic sensitivity analyses to evaluate parameter uncertainty and calculate incremental cost-effectiveness ratios (ICERs) against a £20,000-£30,000 per QALY willingness-to-pay threshold.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first robust economic evaluation of integrated first-trimester risk assessment for the two major complications of twin pregnancy. It will deliver a clear evidence-based recommendation for healthcare policymakers on the value of implementing this screening paradigm. The model will also identify key cost-drivers and outcome measures to guide future research and resource allocation in maternal-fetal medicine.
proposals.novelty: While cost-effectiveness analyses exist for singleton preeclampsia screening, this proposal uniquely integrates the economic impact of screening for both preterm preeclampsia and spontaneous preterm birth within the high-risk context of twin gestation, leveraging Professor Nicolaides's pioneering work in first-trimester risk prediction for both conditions.

proposals.title: A Multicenter Randomized Controlled Trial to Confirm the Reduction in Neonatal Morbidity Following Timed Birth at 39 Weeks in Women at High Risk of Preeclampsia: The CONFIRM Study
proposals.motivation: Preterm birth is a leading cause of neonatal morbidity and mortality. The ASPRE trial and subsequent research on timed birth at term for preeclampsia prevention demonstrated a promising but non-significant reduction in neonatal morbidity, a critical secondary outcome. Confirming this potential benefit in a larger cohort is essential to establish a comprehensive evidence base for this intervention, balancing maternal and neonatal outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: In women identified as high-risk for preeclampsia by first-trimester screening, a policy of planned delivery at 39 weeks of gestation, compared to expectant management until the onset of labor or a medical indication, significantly reduces the composite incidence of serious neonatal morbidity.
proposals.method: We propose a prospective, multicenter, open-label, randomized controlled trial. High-risk women (based on the FMF first-trimester algorithm) will be randomized at 36-37 weeks to either planned delivery at 39 weeks (induction of labor or planned cesarean) or standard care. The primary outcome is a composite of severe neonatal morbidity (e.g., respiratory distress, sepsis, hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy) and perinatal mortality. Using pre-2026 data from the ASPRE trial, a sample size calculation will be performed to achieve 90% power for the previously observed effect size. Analysis will be by intention-to-treat.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide definitive evidence on the neonatal safety and benefit of timed birth at term for preeclampsia prevention. It will either validate the intervention as improving neonatal outcomes or confirm its neutrality, thereby refining clinical guidelines. The results will directly inform the risk-benefit discussions between clinicians and high-risk women in the third trimester.
proposals.novelty: While the ASPRE trial focused on preeclampsia prevention, this study uniquely targets the neonatal morbidity outcome as its primary endpoint, addressing a key evidence gap. It applies the established first-trimester screening paradigm to a novel, larger trial designed specifically to answer a question of neonatal, rather than purely maternal, health.

proposals.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial Comparing Vaginal Progesterone Dose and Formulation for the Prevention of Preterm Birth in Singleton Pregnancies with a Short Cervix: The PROG-DOSE Study
proposals.motivation: Preterm birth remains a leading cause of neonatal morbidity and mortality. While vaginal progesterone is a cornerstone for preventing preterm birth in singletons with a short cervix, pre-2026 evidence is based on heterogeneous trials using different doses (e.g., 90mg, 200mg, 400mg) and formulations (gel, capsule, pessary). This creates clinical uncertainty regarding the optimal regimen for efficacy, tolerability, and patient adherence, hindering standardized care.
proposals.hypothesis: In women with a singleton pregnancy and a mid-trimester cervical length ≤25mm, a higher dose (200mg daily) of micronized vaginal progesterone will be superior to a lower dose (100mg daily) in reducing the rate of spontaneous preterm birth <34 weeks of gestation, with gel and capsule formulations demonstrating equivalent efficacy but differing adherence profiles.
proposals.method: This will be a multicenter, partially blinded, randomized controlled trial with a 2x2 factorial design. Women with a singleton pregnancy and cervical length ≤25mm at 18+0 to 23+6 weeks will be randomized to one of four groups: 100mg capsule, 200mg capsule, 100mg gel, or 200mg gel. The primary outcome is spontaneous preterm birth <34 weeks. Secondary outcomes include gestational age at delivery, neonatal morbidity, maternal side-effects, and adherence measured by returned capsule counts/gel applicators and patient diaries. Analysis will use intention-to-treat, with logistic regression for the primary outcome and interaction testing for dose-formulation effects.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence directly comparing clinically used progesterone doses and formulations within a single trial. It will establish whether a dose-response relationship exists and determine if formulation choice impacts efficacy or adherence, enabling evidence-based, personalized prophylaxis. The findings will directly inform international clinical guidelines for preterm birth prevention.
proposals.novelty: While previous meta-analyses have pooled data from disparate trials, this is the first RCT to use a factorial design to simultaneously isolate the effects of dose and formulation, moving beyond the question of 'if' progesterone works to definitively answer 'how' it should be best prescribed.

proposals.title: A Machine Learning Framework for Individualized Benefit Prediction of Vaginal Progesterone in Women with a Short Cervix to Prevent Preterm Birth
proposals.motivation: Preterm birth (PTB) remains a leading cause of neonatal morbidity. While vaginal progesterone is effective in reducing PTB in women with a short cervix, the treatment effect is heterogeneous, and a significant proportion of treated women still deliver preterm. Current risk stratification is based on a single cervical length threshold, which fails to identify which individuals derive the greatest benefit from intervention, leading to potential overtreatment and missed opportunities for alternative strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a machine learning model integrating first and second-trimester ultrasound biometrics, cervical characteristics, and maternal clinical history can identify distinct phenotypic subgroups with differential treatment responses to vaginal progesterone, enabling precise prediction of individual therapeutic benefit.
proposals.method: We will analyze a large, multi-center cohort of singleton pregnancies with a cervical length ≤25mm between 18-24 weeks. Using pre-treatment data (e.g., cervical length dynamics, funneling, prior PTB, BMI, uterine artery Doppler), we will train a Random Forest model to predict the probability of spontaneous PTB <34 weeks. We will then apply causal forest or meta-learner techniques (e.g., X-learner) on randomized trial data to estimate heterogeneous treatment effects (CATE). Model performance will be validated for calibration and discrimination of treatment benefit.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a clinically actionable tool to move from a 'one-size-fits-all' intervention to a precision medicine approach for PTB prevention. It will define specific patient profiles that derive maximal benefit from progesterone, thereby improving cost-effectiveness and neonatal outcomes, and identify non-responders who may require escalated care (e.g., cerclage).
proposals.novelty: While ML has been used for PTB risk prediction, its application to model heterogeneous treatment effects for progesterone is novel. This approach moves beyond predicting risk to predicting *response to a specific therapy*, a critical step towards personalized antenatal care.

proposals.title: Development of Gestational Age and Maternal Factor-Adjusted Fetal Fraction Thresholds for First-Trimester Cell-Free DNA Screening
proposals.motivation: Cell-free DNA (cfDNA) screening for fetal aneuploidy relies on a fixed fetal fraction (FF) threshold, typically 4%, to determine test reliability. This static threshold fails to account for known biological variability; FF increases with gestational age and is inversely correlated with maternal body mass index (BMI). Consequently, a uniform threshold may lead to unnecessary test failures and delayed results in women with higher BMI or earlier gestation, potentially compromising timely prenatal diagnosis and management, particularly in twin pregnancies where FF dynamics are more complex.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling fetal fraction as a continuous function of gestational age, maternal BMI, and testing platform will yield dynamic, individualized thresholds that reduce test failure rates without compromising test performance, thereby improving screening efficiency and equity.
proposals.method: We will perform a retrospective analysis of prospectively collected individual patient-level data from the Fetal Medicine Foundation’s cfDNA screening database (pre-2026). Using quantile regression, we will model the 2.5th percentile of the FF distribution across gestational weeks (10-13) and maternal BMI categories. The analysis will be stratified by singleton/twin pregnancies and major testing platforms. The derived dynamic thresholds (e.g., 3.2% at 10 weeks, 4.0% at 13 weeks) will be validated on an independent cohort, assessing the reduction in ‘no-call’ rates and the positive predictive value for trisomy 21.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first validated, adjustable FF thresholds for clinical use, directly addressing a key inefficiency in prenatal screening. It will offer evidence-based guidance to optimize cfDNA test reporting, reduce patient anxiety from test failures, and improve screening accessibility for women with higher BMI. The models will be integrated into risk calculation software to personalize screening pathways.
proposals.novelty: While the covariates affecting FF are known, their integration into a formal, data-driven threshold model for clinical decision-making is novel. This moves beyond descriptive observation to create a practical tool that challenges the entrenched paradigm of a single, universal FF cutoff.

proposals.title: The Impact of Maternal Body Mass Index on Cell-Free DNA Test Failure and Screening Performance in Twin Pregnancies: A Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Cell-free DNA (cfDNA) screening for fetal aneuploidy is a cornerstone of prenatal care, but test failure (no-call) rates remain a significant clinical problem. Observational data before 2026 suggest maternal obesity is a key factor for test failure in singleton pregnancies, leading to delayed results and increased patient anxiety. The impact in twin pregnancies, which are at inherently higher risk for aneuploidy and preterm birth, is poorly quantified, creating a critical gap in personalized risk assessment and counseling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that increasing maternal body mass index (BMI) is independently associated with a higher rate of cfDNA test failure and a reduction in test sensitivity for common trisomies in twin pregnancies, and that this relationship modifies the gestational age at which reliable results can be obtained.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using data from the Fetal Medicine Foundation database (2015-2025) on women with twin pregnancies who underwent cfDNA screening (e.g., Harmony, NIPT) between 10 and 14 weeks of gestation. The primary outcome is test failure rate. Secondary outcomes include test sensitivity/specificity for T21, T18, T13, and fetal fraction. We will use multivariable logistic regression to assess the independent effect of maternal BMI (categorized as <25, 25-30, 30-35, >35 kg/m²) on failure, adjusting for gestational age, chorionicity, and maternal age. We will employ receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analysis to determine BMI and gestational age thresholds predictive of failure.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, robust quantification of how maternal weight impacts cfDNA test performance specifically in twins. It will generate evidence-based BMI and gestational age thresholds to guide optimal test timing and pre-test counseling. The findings will directly inform clinical protocols to minimize test failures and delays in a high-risk population, aligning with Professor Nicolaides's focus on precise risk stratification and preterm birth prevention.
proposals.novelty: While the BMI-failure link is recognized in singletons, its detailed characterization in twin gestations—considering chorionicity and its interaction with gestation—is novel. This study moves beyond reporting an association to defining actionable clinical thresholds for test timing, a critical step towards personalized screening pathways in multiple pregnancies.

proposals.title: The UK-Wide Genomic Pregnancy Cohort (UK-GPC): Establishing a Population Registry Linking First-Trimester Cell-Free DNA Screening Data to Perinatal and Long-Term Pediatric Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Cell-free DNA (cfDNA) screening is now a standard prenatal test, providing rich genomic data on fetal fraction, aneuploidy risk, and potentially novel biomarkers. However, its full potential for predicting adverse pregnancy outcomes like preterm birth and preeclampsia, and for understanding long-term child health, remains unrealized due to fragmented data. A large-scale, systematically linked registry is urgently needed to move from isolated risk assessments to a comprehensive life-course understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: That a population-wide registry linking first-trimester cfDNA screening data with detailed pregnancy and pediatric health records will identify novel, early genomic and biomolecular signatures predictive of preterm birth, preeclampsia, and other adverse outcomes, and will reveal associations with long-term neurodevelopmental and cardiometabolic health in children.
proposals.method: We will establish the UK-GPC by prospectively recruiting 100,000 pregnant individuals undergoing routine cfDNA screening at 10-13 weeks. We will link pseudonymized cfDNA results (including fetal fraction, chromosomal profiles, and genome-wide sequencing data where available) to national electronic health records (Hospital Episode Statistics, maternity systems) for pregnancy outcomes (e.g., gestational age at birth, birthweight, preeclampsia). Long-term child follow-up will be achieved via linkage to primary care records and national pediatric datasets. Statistical analysis will employ machine learning algorithms (e.g., penalized regression, random forests) on high-dimensional cfDNA features to build predictive models for preterm birth and preeclampsia, adjusting for established maternal factors.
proposals.contribution: This will create the world's largest resource for investigating the genomic determinants of pregnancy complications, enabling the discovery of new predictive biomarkers. It will provide the first population-scale evidence on the long-term health implications of first-trimester cfDNA profiles, transforming cfDNA from a targeted aneuploidy test into a foundational tool for holistic pregnancy and child health surveillance.
proposals.novelty: While cfDNA is used for aneuploidy screening and some studies link low fetal fraction to adverse outcomes, no pre-2026 initiative systematically links raw cfDNA sequencing data, not just risk calls, to comprehensive, long-term national health records on a population scale to discover novel biomarkers and life-course associations.

proposals.title: Prediction of Cell-Free DNA Fetal Fraction from Maternal Phenotype and Biochemical Markers: A Machine Learning Approach for First-Trimester Risk Assessment
proposals.motivation: Fetal fraction (FF) in maternal plasma cell-free DNA is a critical quality control metric for non-invasive prenatal testing (NIPT) and influences the performance of aneuploidy screening. Low FF (<4%) necessitates repeat sampling, causing patient anxiety and delaying results. In twin pregnancies, FF is even more variable and challenging to interpret. A predictive model for FF could identify women at high risk of low FF early, allowing for optimized sampling timing or counseling, and could improve the interpretation of risk models for preeclampsia and preterm birth that incorporate biochemical markers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that maternal demographic characteristics, biophysical measurements (e.g., BMI, blood pressure), and first-trimester biochemical markers (PAPP-A, free β-hCG) are predictive of the cell-free DNA fetal fraction, and that this relationship differs meaningfully between singleton and twin pregnancies.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of prospectively collected data from a large, well-phenotyped cohort (e.g., the ASPRE trial cohort). The outcome variable is the measured fetal fraction from NIPT at ~10-13 weeks. Predictors will include maternal age, weight, height, BMI, race, smoking status, mean arterial pressure, uterine artery pulsatility index, and serum levels of PAPP-A and free β-hCG. We will develop and compare multiple prediction models: 1) a traditional multivariable linear regression model, 2) a regularized regression (LASSO) to select key features, and 3) a non-linear machine learning model (Gradient Boosting Machine, XGBoost). Model performance will be assessed via cross-validated R-squared and mean absolute error, with separate analyses for singletons and twins.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first validated model to estimate fetal fraction from routine first-trimester assessment data. It will provide clinicians with a tool to anticipate low FF and personalize patient management. Furthermore, by elucidating the maternal determinants of FF, it will contribute to the physiological understanding of placental release of DNA, with implications for refining combined screening algorithms for aneuploidy, preeclampsia, and preterm birth in both singleton and twin gestations.
proposals.novelty: While FF is known to correlate with factors like BMI and gestation, existing work treats it as a confounder. This study inverts the problem, using established maternal traits to predict FF itself as a novel composite biomarker of early placentation. Applying this specifically within the context of a high-risk prevention trial (ASPRE) and for twins is a significant advance.

proposals.title: A Sequential Screening Protocol for Preterm Birth Risk in Twin Pregnancies: A Randomized Controlled Trial of Ultrasound-First Assessment at 19-24 Weeks' Gestation
proposals.motivation: Current first-trimester combined screening for preterm birth (PTB) in twins, integrating maternal factors, cervical length (CL) ultrasound, and biochemical markers, is resource-intensive and may cause unnecessary patient anxiety. A more efficient, cost-effective strategy is needed, particularly for twin pregnancies which carry a significantly elevated risk of PTB. Inverting the traditional sequence could streamline clinical workflow and reduce interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: In women with a twin pregnancy, a sequential screening protocol commencing with a mid-trimester CL ultrasound scan at 19-24 weeks, followed by selective biochemical testing (e.g., fetal fibronectin) only for those with an intermediate CL (15-30mm), will demonstrate non-inferior predictive accuracy for spontaneous PTB <34 weeks compared to universal combined testing, while significantly reducing the need for biochemical tests.
proposals.method: This will be a multicenter, parallel-group, randomized controlled trial. Women with dichorionic twin pregnancies at 18 weeks will be randomized (1:1) to either: 1) Standard Care: combined screening with CL and biochemical testing for all, or 2) Intervention: sequential screening. The intervention arm undergoes CL measurement at 19-24 weeks. Based on pre-2026 risk strata, women are triaged: low-risk (CL >30mm, no further test), high-risk (CL ≤15mm, immediate clinical management), intermediate-risk (CL 15-30mm, offered biochemical testing). The primary outcome is the predictive performance (detection rate and false-positive rate) for spontaneous PTB <34 weeks. Secondary outcomes include cost-analysis, psychological impact assessed via validated questionnaires, and iatrogenic intervention rates.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide high-level evidence for a pragmatic, resource-efficient screening pathway for PTB in twins, directly informing clinical guidelines. It will quantify the potential cost savings and psychological benefits of reducing unnecessary biochemical testing. The findings could redefine the standard of care for antenatal surveillance in multiple pregnancies.
proposals.novelty: While sequential or contingent screening is established in prenatal genetics (e.g., for aneuploidy), its application to PTB risk assessment in twins represents a novel paradigm shift. This inverts the established ASPRE-trial-inspired framework of universal first-trimester combined testing, tailoring the approach to the distinct temporal risk profile of twin gestation.

proposals.title: Optimizing the Timing of Split Biochemical and Ultrasound Screening for Preterm Preeclampsia: A Randomized Controlled Trial
proposals.motivation: The Fetal Medicine Foundation's first-trimester combined screening for preterm preeclampsia, validated in the ASPRE trial, integrates maternal factors, mean arterial pressure, uterine artery pulsatility index, and placental growth factor (PlGF) at 11-13 weeks. However, logistical constraints often necessitate splitting the test, with biochemistry (PlGF) at 9-10 weeks and biophysical markers at 12 weeks. The impact of this temporal separation on screening performance, particularly the risk of biomarker degradation or altered hemodynamics affecting the multivariate Gaussian algorithm's accuracy, is unknown and critical for global implementation.
proposals.hypothesis: That a split screening protocol with biochemistry at 9-10 weeks and ultrasound at 12 weeks yields non-inferior detection and false-positive rates for preterm preeclampsia compared to the standard, simultaneous screening at 11-13 weeks.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multicenter, randomized controlled non-inferiority trial. Women with singleton pregnancies will be randomized to the standard combined screen at 11-13 weeks (Control) or the split protocol (Intervention: PlGF at 9-10 weeks; MAP, UtA-PI, and maternal factors at 12 weeks). The FMF's pre-specified algorithm will calculate risks in both arms. The primary outcome is the detection rate for preterm preeclampsia (<37 weeks) at a 10% false-positive rate. Secondary outcomes include screening performance for early-onset preeclampsia (<34 weeks) and patient acceptability. Sample size will be calculated to establish non-inferiority with a margin of 5%.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence for a standardized, optimized protocol for split first-trimester preeclampsia screening. It will directly inform clinical guidelines, enabling flexible, equitable service delivery without compromising effectiveness. The findings will also refine the underlying risk algorithm by quantifying the temporal stability of its components.
proposals.novelty: While the ASPRE trial validated the combined test, no study has systematically evaluated the real-world scenario of decoupling its components. This research addresses a critical implementation gap by testing the temporal robustness of the multivariate screening model, a non-obvious question given the algorithm's calibration for contemporaneous measurements.

proposals.title: A Multi-Modal Machine Learning Model Integrating First-Trimester Ultrasound, Biochemical, and Maternal Characteristics for the Prediction of Spontaneous Preterm Birth in Twin Pregnancies
proposals.motivation: Preterm birth (PTB) is the leading cause of neonatal morbidity and mortality in twin pregnancies, yet accurate early prediction remains a major clinical challenge. Current screening, such as cervical length measurement, has limited predictive performance when used in isolation. The integration of multi-modal data (ultrasound, biochemistry, maternal history) using advanced analytics offers a promising but underexplored avenue for improving risk stratification in this high-risk cohort.
proposals.hypothesis: A machine learning model integrating first-trimester maternal characteristics, uterine artery pulsatility index (UtA-PI), mean arterial pressure (MAP), pregnancy-associated plasma protein-A (PAPP-A), and placental growth factor (PlGF) will significantly outperform existing single- or dual-marker screening methods for the prediction of spontaneous preterm birth <34 weeks in twin pregnancies.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of prospectively collected data from a large cohort of twin pregnancies undergoing first-trimester combined screening. The dataset will include maternal characteristics, UtA-PI, MAP, PAPP-A, and PlGF. We will train and compare several machine learning algorithms, including logistic regression with regularization (LASSO), random forests, and gradient boosting machines (XGBoost), to identify the optimal model. Model performance will be evaluated using cross-validation and assessed by the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC), calibration, and clinical utility via decision curve analysis.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a validated, multi-modal predictive tool for early PTB risk in twins, directly informing targeted surveillance and potential intervention strategies. It will establish a framework for data integration in twin pregnancy screening and generate hypotheses about the complex, multifactorial pathophysiology of PTB in multiples.
proposals.novelty: While machine learning has been applied to PTB prediction in singletons, its application to the specific and complex context of twin pregnancies using a comprehensive first-trimester multi-modal dataset is novel. This approach moves beyond traditional statistical models to capture non-linear interactions between biomarkers not readily apparent in prior research.

proposals.title: A Feasibility and Impact Study for Universal Implementation of First-Trimester Combined Screening for Preterm Preeclampsia and Spontaneous Preterm Birth in a National Health System
proposals.motivation: Preterm birth and preeclampsia remain leading causes of maternal and neonatal morbidity and mortality. While first-trimester combined screening algorithms for preterm preeclampsia (as validated in the ASPRE trial) and for spontaneous preterm birth (using cervical length and biomarkers) have been developed, they are not yet standard public health practice. Scaling these evidence-based tools to a population level is the critical next step to translate research into widespread clinical benefit, requiring a systematic evaluation of the necessary infrastructure, logistics, and health economic impact.
proposals.hypothesis: A standardized, integrated screening protocol for preterm preeclampsia and spontaneous preterm birth, delivered through existing prenatal care pathways, is logistically feasible at a population level and will lead to a measurable reduction in severe adverse perinatal outcomes through targeted, timely interventions.
proposals.method: The study will design a phased implementation framework within a national health system (e.g., NHS). It will involve: 1) Mapping and integration of existing ultrasound, biomarker, and maternal history data collection at 11-13 weeks gestation into a unified IT platform. 2) Developing and validating a clinical decision-support algorithm that synthesizes risk scores for both conditions and generates standardized management pathways (e.g., aspirin prophylaxis, cervical surveillance). 3) Conducting a prospective, stepped-wedge cluster implementation study across multiple maternity units to evaluate screening uptake, protocol adherence, resource use, and comparative outcomes (rates of preterm preeclampsia <37 weeks and spontaneous preterm birth <34 weeks) against historical controls.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a blueprint for the public health rollout of combined first-trimester screening, including detailed cost-benefit analysis and workflow integration models. It will generate high-quality evidence on the real-world effectiveness of a population-based screening strategy, moving beyond efficacy trials to demonstrate system-wide impact on hard clinical endpoints.
proposals.novelty: While individual screening tests have been studied, this proposal focuses on the systemic integration of two distinct but critical risk assessments into a single, scalable public health program, evaluating the holistic infrastructure required rather than the screening accuracy alone.

proposals.title: The Efficacy of Isolated First-Trimester Nuchal Translucency as a Rapid Triage Tool for Preterm Birth Risk in Twin Pregnancies: A Retrospective Cohort Analysis
proposals.motivation: Preterm birth is the leading cause of perinatal mortality and morbidity in twin pregnancies, necessitating effective early identification for potential interventions. The established combined first-trimester screening for preeclampsia and aneuploidy is resource-intensive, requiring maternal serum biochemistry and specialized software. In low-resource or high-volume settings, a rapid, single-parameter triage tool could prioritize high-risk twins for immediate full assessment and specialist care, optimizing resource allocation.
proposals.hypothesis: In twin pregnancies, first-trimester nuchal translucency (NT) measurement alone, using a predefined threshold (≥95th percentile for gestational age and chorionicity), provides sufficient predictive accuracy for spontaneous preterm birth <34 weeks to serve as an effective rapid triage tool, with performance non-inferior to the full combined screening algorithm for this specific outcome.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using existing databases from the Harris Birthright Research Centre (2010-2025) of dichorionic and monochorionic twin pregnancies with known outcomes. The primary exposure is NT measurement at 11-13+6 weeks, standardized for crown-rump length and chorionicity. The primary outcome is spontaneous preterm birth <34 weeks. We will calculate detection rates, false-positive rates, and likelihood ratios for NT alone at the 95th percentile threshold. Predictive performance will be compared to the full first-trimester combined screening result (which incorporates maternal factors, mean arterial pressure, uterine artery pulsatility index, and serum PAPP-A/PlGF) using receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analysis and net reclassification improvement.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide evidence for a simplified, immediate risk stratification protocol in twin gestation clinics. It could define a clear 'high NT' pathway for intensified surveillance and candidate selection for interventions like cervical cerclage. The findings will inform clinical guidelines for settings where rapid decision-making is critical, potentially reducing the interval between scan and risk assessment.
proposals.novelty: While NT is a known marker for aneuploidy and adverse outcome in singletons, its isolated use as a dedicated triage tool for preterm birth in twins, explicitly decoupled from the full combined screening algorithm, has not been formally validated. This approach shifts the focus from comprehensive risk calculation to immediate binary stratification for a single, critical complication.

proposals.title: Individualized Risk Modeling for Trisomy 21 in the First Trimester: A Machine Learning Approach Integrating Nuchal Translucency, Maternal Age, and Early Ultrasound Phenotype
proposals.motivation: Current first-trimester combined screening for trisomy 21 uses a fixed algorithm based on maternal age, nuchal translucency (NT), and serum biochemistry, which, while effective, may not fully capture complex, non-linear interactions between variables. As prenatal care moves towards more personalized risk assessment, there is a need to leverage modern computational techniques to improve individual risk stratification, potentially enhancing detection rates and reducing unnecessary invasive diagnostic procedures.
proposals.hypothesis: Machine learning models trained on a large, multi-center dataset of first-trimester ultrasound markers and maternal characteristics can generate a more accurate, individualized risk estimate for trisomy 21 than the standard combined test algorithm.
proposals.method: We will utilize a retrospective cohort of >100,000 pregnancies with known outcomes from the Fetal Medicine Foundation database, including NT, crown-rump length, maternal age, and other early ultrasound features (e.g., fetal heart rate, nasal bone). We will train and compare several algorithms pre-2026, including gradient boosting machines (XGBoost), random forests, and neural networks, using nested cross-validation. Model performance will be evaluated against the standard combined test using area under the ROC curve (AUC), detection rate at fixed false-positive rates, and clinical utility via decision curve analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, data-driven framework for individualized trisomy 21 risk assessment that could be integrated into clinical software. It will quantify the potential improvement in screening performance achievable with machine learning, setting a benchmark for future model development and informing clinical guidelines on personalized prenatal screening.
proposals.novelty: While machine learning has been applied to other obstetric outcomes (e.g., preeclampsia in the ASPRE trial), its application to refine the core algorithm for trisomy 21 screening—a cornerstone of Professor Nicolaides's work—represents a novel, logical extension of his risk-based paradigm into the era of advanced computational analytics.

proposals.title: Impact of Universal Nuchal Translucency Measurement on First-Trimester Risk Stratification and Pregnancy Outcomes in a General Obstetric Population: A Stepped-Wedge Cluster Randomized Trial
proposals.motivation: First-trimester nuchal translucency (NT) measurement is a cornerstone of combined screening for aneuploidies and a key marker for major fetal defects and adverse obstetric outcomes, including preterm birth and preeclampsia. However, its integration into routine dating scans in general obstetric practice is inconsistent, creating a two-tiered system where access to comprehensive risk assessment depends on referral to specialist centers. This limits the potential for early, population-wide identification of high-risk pregnancies for targeted monitoring and prevention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematic integration of NT measurement into all first-trimester dating ultrasounds performed in general obstetric practice will improve the early identification of pregnancies at high risk for aneuploidies, major fetal anomalies, and adverse outcomes like preterm birth and preeclampsia, without reducing scan quality or increasing maternal anxiety.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pragmatic, stepped-wedge cluster randomized trial across a network of general obstetric ultrasound practices. Clusters (practices) will be randomized to transition from their current standard (dating scan only) to the intervention (dating scan + standardized NT measurement) at different time points. The primary outcome will be the detection rate of pregnancies flagged as high-risk (using established pre-2026 algorithms combining NT, biochemistry, and maternal factors) for aneuploidy, preeclampsia (ASPRE-like algorithm), and spontaneous preterm birth (<34 weeks). Secondary outcomes will include scan duration, technical success rate of NT measurement, rates of subsequent invasive testing, and patient-reported anxiety scores. Analysis will use mixed-effects models to account for clustering and time trends.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide high-level evidence on the feasibility, effectiveness, and broader clinical utility of scaling up first-trimester NT screening to a general population setting. It will quantify the impact on risk stratification for multiple conditions beyond aneuploidy and inform national policy on the standardization of first-trimester ultrasound. The findings will directly address the practical challenges of moving specialized screening protocols into routine care.
proposals.novelty: While NT's value in specialist screening is proven, this trial tests the novel concept of its systematic, scalable integration into non-specialist, routine care as a multifunctional public health tool, evaluating real-world effectiveness across a spectrum of outcomes central to Prof. Nicolaides's research on preterm birth and preeclampsia prevention.

ideas.title: Develop a dynamic, marker-specific risk threshold framework for prenatal screening
ideas.description: This research aims to integrate the investigation of novel biochemical analytes with the optimization of clinical risk thresholds by developing a model where the 'optimal' cutoff for recommending invasive diagnostic testing is conditional on the specific biomarker panel result. The study will determine if different combinations or magnitudes of marker deviations (both established and novel) should trigger different risk thresholds, moving beyond a one-size-fits-all approach. This would allow for a more nuanced balance between detection rate and false-positive rate that is tailored to an individual's biochemical profile, potentially improving clinical utility and ethical acceptability. The core innovation is a shift from seeking a single best population threshold to creating an adaptive, evidence-based decision framework that personalizes the risk interpretation based on the multi-analyte signature.

It posits that the parental drive for a definitive diagnosis can be satisfied by developing a continuous 'fetal health index' derived from existing and novel biomarkers. This enables clinicians to provide parents with definitive, ongoing information about fetal well-being and potentially guide preemptive nutritional or lifestyle interventions to improve health outcomes, irrespective of aneuploidy status.

ideas.title: From Screening to Management: Prenatal Testing as a Platform for Postnatal Care Planning
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that prenatal screening's sole goal is to guide invasive testing for termination by drawing an analogy to oncology. In cancer screening, a positive result initiates a broad, risk-stratified management pathway, not just a decision for immediate intervention. Adapting this solution, the research proposes to reframe prenatal screening as the entry point to a longitudinal 'condition management pathway.' Following a screen-positive result, the process would automatically engage resources to develop a personalized postnatal care plan involving pediatric specialists, early intervention services, and family support. This model provides valuable, actionable information for all parents—whether they choose to continue the pregnancy or not—shifting the focus from a single decision point to improving long-term preparedness and outcomes, thereby increasing the utility and ethical grounding of screening programs.

ideas.title: Fetal Health Biomarker Constellation Analysis via Multi-omics Deviation Mapping
ideas.description: This idea inverts the problem by using marker deviations to indicate general fetal well-being, not just aneuploidy. It draws an analogy from astrophysics, where exoplanets are detected indirectly through their systemic effects on a host star (e.g., wobble, dimming). The analogous solution is to infer properties of an unseen system by modeling coordinated deviations in a measurable proxy. Adapted to prenatal screening, the research idea is to develop a 'Fetal Health Biomarker Constellation Analysis' model. This model would use high-dimensional multi-omics data from maternal blood to detect a pattern of subtle, correlated deviations from a healthy normative baseline. The integrated signature of these deviations—their constellation—would provide a quantitative assessment of general fetal physiologic state, screening for conditions like growth restriction or hypoxic stress, thereby moving beyond a binary aneuploidy check.

ideas.title: Adapting Portfolio Optimization Models to Evaluate First-Trimester Screening Cost-Effectiveness
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from financial portfolio optimization to evaluate the cost-effectiveness of first-trimester screening. In portfolio theory, quantitative models are used to find the optimal allocation of resources across assets, balancing risk and return. Analogously, different prenatal screening strategies and their sequences can be modeled as a portfolio of 'health investments.' The adapted idea is to develop a health economic model that treats screening tests as assets with costs, detection rates (sensitivity/specificity), and downstream consequences. Using optimization techniques from finance, the research will identify the efficient frontier of screening strategies, determining the most cost-effective 'portfolio' (e.g., a combined first-trimester approach, perhaps with contingent follow-up) that maximizes health outcomes like quality-adjusted life years (QALYs) for a given healthcare budget, or minimizes total cost for a required performance threshold, providing a rigorous, quantitative framework for policy decision-making.

ideas.title: Adapting Survival Analysis Censoring for cfDNA Test Validation with Incomplete Outcome Data
ideas.description: This idea adapts the well-established statistical concept of right-censoring from survival analysis in clinical trials to the challenge of validating cfDNA test performance with incomplete pregnancy outcome data. In survival studies, patients who haven't experienced the event by the study end are 'censored' but still inform the analysis. The analogous solution is to treat a confirmed prenatal or postnatal diagnostic outcome as the 'event' and model the 'time' to that verification. The adapted research idea is to develop a statistical framework (e.g., a time-to-verification analysis) where subjects with missing final outcomes are incorporated as censored observations. This allows the use of all collected data, including partial follow-up, to estimate test performance characteristics like sensitivity and specificity, thereby relaxing the stringent and often impractical requirement for complete outcome data on 100% of the screened cohort.

Using participatory design with demographically and ideologically diverse cohorts, it identifies distinct user archetypes and their associated informational and decisional needs. The output is a prototype for an adaptive clinical decision-support platform that tailors information delivery, counseling resources, and follow-up options based on parent-stated preferences gathered at the pre-test stage, thereby aligning screening practice with a pluralistic model of reproductive autonomy.

ideas.title: Economic modeling for the incremental value of cfDNA-based microdeletion screening in general prenatal care
ideas.description: This research project integrates advanced economic modeling with the technical development of cfDNA-based microdeletion detection to answer a pivotal policy question. It will develop a decision-analytic model (e.g., Markov model or cost-effectiveness analysis) that incorporates variables such as the performance characteristics (sensitivity, specificity) of the microdeletion assay, population prevalence of various sub-chromosomal CNVs, downstream healthcare costs of diagnosed versus undiagnosed conditions, and patient/parental quality-of-life outcomes. The core innovation is using the economic model to back-solve for the minimum required technical performance (e.g., positive predictive value thresholds) that a microdeletion screening method must achieve to be cost-effective in a general screening context, thereby providing clear, evidence-based targets for assay development and guiding responsible implementation.

ideas.title: Adapting Environmental Calibration Methods to Control for Maternal Biological Noise in cfDNA Testing
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from environmental remote sensing, where atmospheric interference is calibrated out of satellite data, to the problem of maternal biological conditions confounding cfDNA test performance. The analogous solution involves modeling the interference source using control data and multi-parameter analysis. The adapted idea is to treat conditions like maternal obesity or autoimmune disease as sources of biological 'noise' that increase total cfDNA, alter fragmentation patterns, or introduce confounding genomic signals. By developing a pre-analytical and computational calibration framework—using maternal plasma proteomics, cell-free DNA methylation signatures from paired maternal buffy coat or tissue samples, and fragment size distribution analysis—the research aims to create condition-specific correction algorithms. This would allow for the subtraction of the maternal cfDNA background, improving the fidelity of the fetal signal and the diagnostic accuracy of non-invasive prenatal testing across diverse maternal populations.

ideas.title: Optimizing the Clinical Risk Cutoff Using Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) and Decision Curve Analysis from Diagnostic Test Evaluation
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the solution from diagnostic test evaluation, specifically Decision Curve Analysis (DCA), to the problem of determining the most appropriate risk threshold for recommending invasive diagnostic procedures. In the analogous field, DCA is used to find the optimal probability cutoff for a diagnostic test by balancing sensitivity and specificity with the clinical consequences of incorrect calls. The adapted idea applies this by constructing a decision-analytic model that calculates the net benefit of performing an invasive test (e.g., biopsy, catheterization) across a range of pre-test probability thresholds. It explicitly incorporates patient-derived utilities for health outcomes and the harms of unnecessary procedures, moving beyond statistical optimization to find the threshold that is clinically and ethically appropriate by maximizing overall patient welfare.

ideas.title: Incorporate a multi-analyte panel using machine learning feature selection from proteomics
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from astrophysical spectroscopy, where machine learning analyzes thousands of spectral lines to detect faint exoplanet signals. The analogous problem in prenatal screening is improving detection while reducing false positives from a complex biological background. The adapted research idea is to employ high-throughput proteomic/metabolomic profiling of maternal serum to generate a large multi-analyte dataset. Machine learning feature selection techniques will then be applied to this dataset to identify the most predictive combination of novel and existing biochemical markers, defining an optimized panel that significantly improves screening performance over current multi-marker tests.

ideas.title: A Bayesian Fragmentomics Network for Sequential, Multi-Feature cfDNA-Based Prenatal Risk Assessment
ideas.description: This research direction fuses the probabilistic reasoning of Bayesian networks with the multi-analyte approach of liquid biopsy fragmentomics for non-invasive prenatal testing (NIPT). It constructs a causal network where nodes represent different cfDNA features (fragment length distributions, methylation patterns, nucleosome footprints) and their relationships to fetal ploidy and maternal confounding factors. As sequential test results from a maternal blood sample become available, the network dynamically updates the posterior probability of aneuploidy, synthesizing weak individual signals into a robust composite diagnosis. This enables a new paradigm of 'adaptive NIPT,' where the order and combination of assays can be optimized in silico to reach a diagnostic confidence threshold with minimal cost and time.

ideas.title: Inverse Problem: Deduce Maternal Health from cfDNA via Geophysical Tomography Methods
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to inverse problems from geophysical seismic tomography to the challenge of deducing undiagnosed maternal conditions from anomalous cell-free DNA (cfDNA) screening results. In geophysics, hidden subsurface structures are inferred by solving an inverse problem using a forward model of wave propagation and observed surface anomalies. Here, we treat the maternal physiological state as the hidden structure and the cfDNA in maternal plasma as the observed surface data. By building a detailed forward model that predicts cfDNA characteristics (fragmentomics, methylation, copy number) from a map of healthy maternal and fetal tissues, we can computationally invert clinically observed anomalies. The mismatch between predicted and observed cfDNA profiles is used to iteratively adjust the model of maternal tissue states, revealing signatures of occult malignancies, autoimmune flares, or other systemic conditions that alter cellular turnover and DNA release patterns.

ideas.title: Bayesian Multi-Source Validation Framework for cfDNA Performance with Non-Ignorable Missing Outcomes
ideas.description: This research develops a robust statistical framework to validate cfDNA test performance when pregnancy outcome follow-up is incomplete and non-randomly missing. The method integrates cfDNA results, partially observed clinical outcomes, and auxiliary data from linked registries or early clinical markers within a Bayesian model that explicitly parameterizes the outcome-dependent missingness mechanism. This approach probabilistically imputes missing true outcomes and propagates the associated uncertainty into all performance estimates. The final output provides validated performance metrics with credible intervals that honestly reflect the information loss due to missing data, enabling reliable use of incomplete datasets while rigorously controlling for verification bias.

ideas.title: Adapting 'Informed Consent Stress Models' from Clinical Trial Ethics to Map Parental Psychological Trajectories in Newborn Screening
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from clinical trial ethics, where the psychological impact of complex informed consent processes is rigorously studied. The analogous field uses longitudinal designs to map participant stress trajectories and test interventions to mitigate anxiety. The adapted idea applies this to newborn screening by conducting a prospective cohort study to chart the long-term psychological trajectory of parents through the entire screening and diagnostic odyssey. It will identify critical high-anxiety decision points and empirically test the effectiveness of targeted supportive interventions, such as structured genetic counseling sessions or interactive digital information tools, on reducing parental anxiety and improving long-term psychological adjustment without undermining the value of screening.

ideas.title: Adapting Signal Amplification from Gravitational Wave Detection to Low-Fetal-Fraction NIPT
ideas.description: This idea inverts the assumption that a minimum fetal fraction is sufficient for reliable NIPT by addressing the core challenge of low-FF cases. Drawing an analogy from gravitational wave astronomy, which uses matched filtering to extract faint signals from noise, we propose adapting a template-matching solution. The research would involve creating a comprehensive library of expected cfDNA fragmentation patterns, methylation profiles, and haplotype structures specific to maternal and fetal genomes. For a low-FF sample, the mixed signal would be computationally compared against this template library. The correlation and residual analysis would effectively amplify the fetal component, potentially allowing for confident screening in cases where current methods fail or require redraws, thereby expanding the test's reliable applicability.

ideas.title: Adapting Portfolio Theory to Predict Polygenic Traits from cfDNA
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea by drawing an analogy from financial economics. The analogous problem is predicting the behavior of a complex portfolio composed of many individual, fluctuating assets, which was solved by Modern Portfolio Theory through covariance-based aggregation of component statistics. The adapted idea investigates if cell-free DNA (cfDNA) can be used to predict complex polygenic fetal traits by modeling the maternal plasma cfDNA mixture not as a source of distinct fetal genotypes, but as a 'portfolio' of faint fetal genetic signals. The solution involves adapting MPT's framework: using known genome-wide association study (GWAS) effect sizes and linkage disequilibrium (covariance) structures for the trait, a model will be developed to aggregate the myriad weak, partial signals from fetal single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) present in the cfDNA to estimate the fetus's aggregate genetic predisposition, moving beyond the detection of aneuploidies to quantitative polygenic risk prediction.

ideas.title: Dynamic Calibration of Screening Models Using Real-Time Process Control in Diverse Populations
ideas.description: This research integrates statistical process control (SPC) from manufacturing into the validation of a multi-modal screening model across diverse ethnic and geographic cohorts. Instead of a one-time validation, SPC charts are used to continuously monitor the consistency of input measurements (biochemical and ultrasound) and the model's output predictions within each population subgroup over time. This allows for the early detection of 'process drift'—where changing demographics, regional practices, or assay reagents cause the model's performance to degrade for specific groups. The synergy enables a dynamic, equity-focused calibration framework where validation is an ongoing, monitored process, and corrective adjustments to the model or measurement protocols can be proactively implemented to maintain generalizability and fairness.

ideas.title: Population-Aware Micro-Deletion Detection: Validating cfDNA Screening for Rare CNVs Across Diverse Ancestries
ideas.description: This research direction integrates the technical challenge of detecting rare, sub-chromosomal copy number variants (CNVs) from cfDNA with a rigorous validation framework across diverse, global populations. The core synergy is to systematically investigate how population-specific genomic architecture—including reference genome biases, polymorphic copy number regions, and unique haplotype structures—impacts the sensitivity, specificity, and positive predictive value of micro-deletion screening. By co-developing detection algorithms and validation cohorts from the outset, the project will generate ancestrally informed bioinformatics pipelines and establish ethnicity-specific performance metrics, moving beyond a one-size-fits-all model. This will provide the foundational data needed to understand the generalizability and limitations of advanced non-invasive prenatal testing for rare genomic disorders in a global context.

ideas.title: Causal Audit and Targeted Adaptation of Screening Model Across Populations
ideas.description: This research implements an active causal audit framework to move beyond simply measuring performance disparities in diverse populations. It employs techniques like counterfactual analysis and bias decomposition to identify the root causes—such as differences in disease presentation, access to care, or biomarker baselines—driving any loss of model accuracy. Based on these causal findings, the model undergoes targeted, scientifically-justified adaptations, which may include subgroup-specific calibration or the incorporation of contextually informative features. The final output is a rigorously adapted screening model accompanied by a transparent report detailing the sources of disparity and the evidence for each adaptation, ensuring robust and equitable generalizability.

ideas.title: A Bayesian Hierarchical Framework for Quantifying and Modeling Population-Specific Heterogeneity in Multi-Ethnic cfDNA Validation Studies
ideas.description: This research direction integrates advanced Bayesian hierarchical meta-analysis with large-scale, multi-ethnic validation studies. It moves beyond simply reporting aggregate or stratified performance metrics by using the hierarchical model to explicitly treat different ethnic and geographic cohorts as related but distinct groups within a unified probabilistic framework. This allows for the joint estimation of a global performance parameter while quantifying between-population heterogeneity, borrowing statistical strength across groups where appropriate. The outcome is a calibrated model that can predict the likely performance and uncertainty of the cfDNA screening method in new target populations, providing a powerful tool for health policy and implementation planning.

ideas.title: Modeling with Embedded Safeguards: A Prospective Study of Decision Quality and Societal Dynamics in Population cfDNA Screening
ideas.description: This research embeds a rigorous, prospective study of informed decision-making within the logistical modeling of a population-wide cfDNA screening program. It employs longitudinal surveys and discrete choice experiments to quantitatively measure autonomy, understanding, and values concordance among pregnant individuals offered the test. Concurrently, an agent-based model integrates these empirical data on decision-making behavior to simulate long-term societal outcomes under different implementation models, explicitly testing policy safeguards designed to prevent routinization and coercion.

ideas.title: Personalized, Utility-Based Decision Support for Invasive Diagnostic Testing
ideas.description: This research develops and validates a clinical decision support system that generates personalized testing recommendations instead of applying a population-level risk threshold. The system uses a formal decision-analytic model that incorporates patient-specific clinical data, explicit utilities for potential outcomes (e.g., procedural complications, delayed diagnosis), and individual risk preferences elicited through validated tools. It outputs a tailored recommendation alongside a clear visualization of the trade-offs, enabling shared decision-making that is both clinically sound and ethically grounded in patient autonomy and values.

ideas.title: Implement a Geographically Stratified, Consortium-Based Validation Framework
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from climate science's method for validating global climate models, this idea proposes a structured, consortium-based approach to test the screening model's generalizability. In climate science, projects like the Coupled Model Intercomparison Project (CMIP) use standardized protocols to test models across diverse, pre-defined geographic and climatic strata. Adapted to this medical context, an international consortium would be established to validate the combined screening method. The consortium would implement a common evaluation protocol across multiple, independent research sites, each representing a distinct population stratum defined by ethnicity, geographic region, and local epidemiological factors. This coordinated, stratified validation design allows for a systematic assessment of performance variation, robust confirmation of generalizability, and identification of any sub-populations requiring model adjustment.

ideas.title: Adapting Universal Vaccination Campaigns to Population cfDNA Screening
ideas.description: This research seed expands by drawing an analogy to the field of Public Health & Infectious Disease Epidemiology, specifically the historical challenge of scaling childhood vaccination programs to a universal population level. The analogous solution involved phased geographic rollouts, standardized education/consent protocols, centralized data tracking, and ethical opt-out mechanisms. Adapted to the research context, the idea is to model the logistical and ethical implications of offering cfDNA screening to all pregnant individuals by designing and simulating a tiered implementation framework. This model will incorporate a staged rollout strategy, a unified national protocol for pre-test counseling and informed consent, a proposed central data registry to track screening uptake, results, and pregnancy outcomes, and a comparative ethical analysis of different population-scale consent models (opt-in vs. opt-out) to balance autonomy with public health benefit.

It will develop simulation models to compare the cost-effectiveness of this robust, low-resource integrated pathway against traditional high-precision integrated screening and cfDNA-first strategies in unselected populations. The analysis will quantify how much ultrasound precision can be relaxed before eroding the economic advantage of integrated screening, identifying thresholds for viable implementation in varied clinical environments. The outcome will be evidence-based protocols that optimize the balance of cost, accessibility, and screening performance for different resource levels.

ideas.title: Adapting Blind Source Separation from Audio Signal Processing to Deconvolve cfDNA
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core solution from Blind Source Separation in audio signal processing to the problem of deconvolving mixed cfDNA signals. In acoustics, techniques like Independent Component Analysis separate individual sound sources from a single mixed recording by identifying statistically independent components. Adapted to cfDNA analysis, the mixed plasma sequencing data—containing reads from both maternal and fetal genomes—is treated as the composite signal. Computational methods are developed to apply BSS/ICA principles to genomic features within the data, such as allelic ratios, fragmentomics, or epigenetic marks, to statistically isolate the fetal genomic contribution from the maternal background. This provides a deconvolution method that does not rely on paternal genotyping or large reference panels, analogous to 'blindly' separating speakers in a room from one microphone recording.

ideas.title: Adaptive, patient-specific anomaly detection for longitudinal biomarker monitoring
ideas.description: This system moves beyond static control charts by implementing a Bayesian framework for online learning and change point detection tailored to each patient's unique physiological trajectory. It continuously updates a dynamic model of expected values for biochemical and ultrasound measurements, incorporating known time-dependent factors like recovery phases or disease progression. Anomalies are flagged based on significant deviations from this personalized, evolving baseline rather than a fixed population average. This approach minimizes false alarms from expected biological variation while increasing sensitivity to patient-specific pathological shifts, enabling more reliable and actionable clinical monitoring.

ideas.title: Fragment-Level Epigenetic Deconvolution of cfDNA Using Pseudo-Cell Probabilistic Clustering
ideas.description: This approach reconceptualizes the deconvolution problem by treating each short cfDNA fragment as an independent observation with associated epigenetic and fragmentation metadata, analogous to a single cell. A multi-modal Bayesian clustering model integrates fragment length distributions, cell-type-specific methylation densities inferred from sparse CpG coverage, nucleosome positioning signals, and preferred end motifs to assign each fragment a probability of originating from fetal, maternal, or other cellular sources. This pre-variant-calling separation creates purified, cell-type-enriched fragment pools, dramatically increasing the signal-to-noise ratio for subsequent fetal genotyping and aneuploidy detection from maternal plasma.

ideas.title: Bayesian Network Ensemble Learning with Causal Discovery for Adaptive Diagnostic Risk Stratification
ideas.description: This system learns the diagnostic Bayesian network's structure and parameters directly from multimodal patient data, rather than relying on a pre-specified, static model. It employs causal discovery techniques to infer potential directed relationships and uses nonparametric Bayesian methods to flexibly estimate conditional distributions. By maintaining a posterior distribution over possible network structures, the system dynamically updates risk probabilities as sequential test results arrive while explicitly quantifying the uncertainty inherent in the model itself. This results in more robust, data-driven, and less biased risk assessments that adapt as more population-level evidence is accumulated.

ideas.title: Consistency-Regularized Multi-Source Learning for Robust NT Assessment with Variable-Quality Inputs
ideas.description: This research develops a machine learning system that maintains clinical-grade accuracy even when using less precise nuchal translucency (NT) measurements from varied operators. The core innovation is a dual-branch neural network trained on a large, accessible dataset of variable-quality scans and a smaller, expert-annotated high-precision dataset. A consistency regularization loss function forces the model's predictions from the imprecise input branch to align with the high-fidelity branch's understanding of anatomical norms and risk correlations. This approach ensures the model's robustness to measurement noise without sacrificing its ability to identify the subtle biometric features that are clinically meaningful for aneuploidy screening.

ideas.title: Causally-constrained, interpretable machine learning for integrated biomarker and NT risk modeling
ideas.description: This research develops a hybrid analytical framework that integrates causal biological knowledge with advanced pattern recognition to create robust and interpretable risk models. The system first uses established pathophysiological understanding to define a directed acyclic graph (DAG) constraining plausible relationships between biomarkers, neuroimaging traits (NT), and clinical outcomes. Machine learning algorithms, such as grouped lasso within structural equation models or causal forest techniques, are then applied to learn non-linear interaction effects and refine weights strictly within these biologically plausible pathways. The result is a risk score decomposed into contributions from distinct mechanistic modules (e.g., inflammation, neurodegeneration), ensuring the model is both predictive and clinically interpretable for stratified decision-making.

Adapted to medical risk integration, pattern recognition algorithms (e.g., ensemble methods, Bayesian neural networks) would be configured to treat various biomarkers and NT data as independent probabilistic inputs. The model would learn to weight and combine these inputs within a structured framework, analogous to a logic tree, to generate a composite, uncertainty-aware risk score for a clinical outcome, thereby providing a robust method for multi-source risk integration.
